road.name: WA-20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: seasonal; open roughly late April/May through November
road.seasonalGate: Closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary year to year with snowpack); winter closure gates sit near Ross Dam TH (west side) and Mazama (east side).
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount, Newhalem, and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass — roughly 170-180 miles, about 3-3.5 hours nonstop. From the east side, it's a short 20-30 minute drive west from Winthrop/Mazama on SR-20.

routes.approach: Access is the same as the rest of the Liberty Bell Group: from Seattle via SR-20 through Marblemount and Rainy Pass, or from Winthrop/Mazama a short drive west, to the Blue Lake Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass required) about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass. Hike the Blue Lake Trail about 1.5 miles, then take the climbers' path left up through slabs and talus into the west basin beneath the spires. For South Early Winters Spire — the highest summit of the group — continue further up the basin on scree/snow gullies toward the South Arete or Southwest Couloir routes rather than cutting off at the Liberty Bell/Concord notch, roughly 3-3.5 miles and 2,000+ ft of gain, about 2-3 hours to the base of the route. For this specific route: from the basin, follow climbers' trails through the alpine meadows to the base of the SW Rib, marked by a distinctive Y-shaped larch tree at the southwest corner of the spire; several of these routes share this same start or a variation of it nearby.

For this specific route: from the basin, leave the climbers' trail at a minor stream/cairn and work up through slabs and open country parallel to the stream to reach the low end of the ridge at the southwest corner of the spire, a slightly higher and more southerly point than the SW Rib routes' base.

routes.approach: Access is the same as the rest of the Liberty Bell Group: from Seattle via SR-20 through Marblemount and Rainy Pass, or from Winthrop/Mazama a short drive west, to the Blue Lake Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass required) about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass. Hike the Blue Lake Trail about 1.5 miles, then take the climbers' path left up through slabs and talus into the west basin beneath the spires. For South Early Winters Spire — the highest summit of the group — continue further up the basin on scree/snow gullies toward the South Arete or Southwest Couloir routes rather than cutting off at the Liberty Bell/Concord notch, roughly 3-3.5 miles and 2,000+ ft of gain, about 2-3 hours to the base of the route. For this specific route: from the same basin, continue scrambling further around the south face toward the notch between North and South Early Winters Spire, then traverse to the base of the East Buttress. Some parties instead approach this specific route from the opposite (east) side via the SR-20 hairpin turnout and Spire Gully.

For this specific route: from the basin, follow climbers' trails through the alpine meadows to the base of the SW Rib, marked by a distinctive Y-shaped larch tree at the southwest corner of the spire; several of these routes share this same start or a variation of it nearby.

road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass — roughly 170-180 miles, about 3-3.5 hours nonstop. From Winthrop/Mazama on the east side it's a short 20-30 minute drive west on SR-20.

routes.approach: Same access as the rest of the Liberty Bell Group: from Seattle via SR-20 over Rainy Pass, or a short drive from Winthrop/Mazama, to the Blue Lake Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass required) about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass. Follow the Blue Lake Trail about 1.5 miles, then branch onto the climbers' path into the west basin below the massif. Most North Early Winters Spire routes (the technically hardest of the group, none easier than 5.7) are on the west side, reached by continuing up basin gullies past where the Liberty Bell/Concord notch route splits off — figure roughly 3 miles and 2,000+ ft of gain, about 2-3 hours of steep hiking and 3rd-class scrambling to the base.

routes.approach: This route does NOT use the Blue Lake Trailhead, unlike this spire's other routes. Park at the hairpin-turn pullout on SR-20 (roughly 0.5mi east of Washington Pass, beneath the east faces of the Early Winter Spires). Ascend the gully between North and South Early Winter Spire, staying right of the gully divide, and scramble to a flat ledge at the base of the route.

road.name: SR-20 hairpin pullout (east side of Washington Pass)
road.status: Paved highway pullout, no formal trailhead facilities — distinct from the Blue Lake Trailhead used for this spire's other routes
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April
road.driveNote: From Seattle via SR-20 through Marblemount and Rainy Pass, or from Winthrop/Mazama a short drive west, park at the hairpin-turn pullout on the east side of Washington Pass — not the Blue Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: Same access as the rest of the Liberty Bell Group: from Seattle via SR-20 over Rainy Pass, or a short drive from Winthrop/Mazama, to the Blue Lake Trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass required) about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass. Follow the Blue Lake Trail about 1.5 miles, then branch onto the climbers' path into the west basin below the massif. Most North Early Winters Spire routes (the technically hardest of the group, none easier than 5.7) are on the west side, reached by continuing up basin gullies past where the Liberty Bell/Concord notch route splits off — figure roughly 3 miles and 2,000+ ft of gain, about 2-3 hours of steep hiking and 3rd-class scrambling to the base.

road.status: seasonal; open roughly late April/May through November
road.seasonalGate: Closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary year to year with snowpack); winter closure gates sit near Ross Dam TH (west side) and Mazama (east side).
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass — roughly 170-180 miles, about 3-3.5 hours nonstop. From Winthrop/Mazama on the east side it's a short 20-30 minute drive west on SR-20.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive SR-20 through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass (or approach from Winthrop/Mazama on the east side, a short drive west); park at the Blue Lake Trailhead (about 5,200 ft, Northwest Forest Pass required), about 1.5 miles west of the pass. Hike the Blue Lake Trail about 1.5 miles to a meadow, then take the climbers' path up through slabs, talus, and meadow into the basin below the Liberty Bell Group, with short 3rd-class scrambling sections — roughly 3 miles and 2,000 ft of gain, 1.5-2.5 hours. Concord Tower routes (South Face, North Face, Tunnel Route) start from the steep gully and notch (about 7,300 ft) between Liberty Bell and Concord Tower; this gully is often snow-filled and icy into late June, with rockfall risk after melt-out. IMPORTANT — this route uses a different notch than Concord Tower's North Face routes: follow Blue Lake Trail roughly 2mi (farther than the north-side turnoff) to where the trail opens up, then a separate climbers' trail marked by a large cairn leads up the Concord-Lexington gully to the notch between Concord Tower and Lexington Tower — south of, and reached differently than, the Liberty Bell-Concord notch.

routes.approach: Summertime Crag uses a different trailhead than the other four areas' shared Blue Lake approach. From Winthrop, drive west on SR-20 through Mazama toward Washington Pass (or, coming from Seattle, continue past Washington Pass toward Mazama on SR-20); park at a small pullout on the east side of the highway, just before the larger 'Spires Gully' parking area used to access the Early Winters Spires from the east. From the pullout, descend off the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it briefly downstream, then climb up-slope and contour left to reach the crag — about a 20-25 minute walk with minimal elevation change. The crag stays shaded until mid-afternoon in summer, making it a popular warm-weather alternative when Mazama-area cragging runs hot.

road.name: WA-20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: seasonal; open roughly late April/May through November
road.seasonalGate: Closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary year to year with snowpack); winter closure gates sit near Ross Dam TH (west side) and Mazama (east side).
road.driveNote: Most commonly reached from the east: from Winthrop, drive west on SR-20 through Mazama toward Washington Pass. From Seattle it's the same SR-20 corridor through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass, continuing past Washington Pass toward Mazama — roughly 180+ miles, about 3.5-4 hours nonstop.

road.seasonalGate: Closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary year to year with snowpack); winter closure gates sit near Ross Dam TH (west side) and Mazama (east side).
road.driveNote: Most commonly reached from the east: from Winthrop, drive west on SR-20 through Mazama toward Washington Pass. From Seattle it's the same SR-20 corridor through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass, continuing past Washington Pass toward Mazama — roughly 180+ miles, about 3.5-4 hours nonstop.

routes.approach: Summertime Crag uses a different trailhead than the other four areas' shared Blue Lake approach. From Winthrop, drive west on SR-20 through Mazama toward Washington Pass (or, coming from Seattle, continue past Washington Pass toward Mazama on SR-20); park at a small pullout on the east side of the highway, just before the larger 'Spires Gully' parking area used to access the Early Winters Spires from the east. From the pullout, descend off the road down through trees, cross Early Winters Creek, follow it briefly downstream, then climb up-slope and contour left to reach the crag — about a 20-25 minute walk with minimal elevation change. The crag stays shaded until mid-afternoon in summer, making it a popular warm-weather alternative when Mazama-area cragging runs hot.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north and then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east through Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, and Rockport to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road south for about 20 miles — paved for the first 10 miles, gravel and washboard the rest of the way — to the Eldorado parking area near mile 20. From the trailhead, hikers backtrack a short distance along the road to find a safe crossing of the Cascade River (historically a large log or braided ford, which shifts year to year), then pick up a steep, unmaintained climbers' boot path that gains roughly 3,000-3,500 feet through old-growth forest to a boulder field, crosses Eldorado Creek, and opens into the alpine meadows of the Eldorado Creek basin, a common camp area roughly 5-6 hours from the car. From camp, the route continues onto glaciated terrain (roped travel and crevasse-rescue gear required) climbing toward Eldorado's famous narrow snow ridge and the 8,868-foot summit, typically another 3-5 hours. Any overnight stay requires a free North Cascades National Park backcountry permit — Eldorado is managed as a high-use cross-country zone with advance reservations available on Recreation.gov plus a limited walk-up allocation issued in person at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center. For this specific route: from Eldorado/Roush Creek high camp, continue up snowfields onto the Eldorado Glacier to the summit ridge system; West Ridge traverses further around past the Triad to the west face before ascending, but all three routes stay on Eldorado Peak itself.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally; paved for the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then rough gravel/washboard with potholes for the remaining ~13 miles to the road's end at Cascade Pass Trailhead (mile 23.1). The Eldorado parking area/trailhead is at roughly mile 20. Washouts and ruts are common and can require high-clearance vehicles; check current NPS road-status postings before driving.
road.seasonalGate: No permanent gate, but the road is not maintained through winter/spring snowpack and typically doesn't fully open to its end until late June or early July; storm damage can delay opening further.
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 north to the WA-20 exit near Burlington, then east on WA-20 through Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, and Rockport to Marblemount (roughly 1.5-2 hours). At Marblemount, turn right (south) onto Cascade River Road and continue about 20 miles to the Eldorado parking area — the last ~10 miles are gravel, so allow extra time.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north and then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east through Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, and Rockport to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road south for about 20 miles — paved for the first 10 miles, gravel and washboard the rest of the way — to the Eldorado parking area near mile 20. From the trailhead, hikers backtrack a short distance along the road to find a safe crossing of the Cascade River (historically a large log or braided ford, which shifts year to year), then pick up a steep, unmaintained climbers' boot path that gains roughly 3,000-3,500 feet through old-growth forest to a boulder field, crosses Eldorado Creek, and opens into the alpine meadows of the Eldorado Creek basin, a common camp area roughly 5-6 hours from the car. From camp, the route continues onto glaciated terrain (roped travel and crevasse-rescue gear required) climbing toward Eldorado's famous narrow snow ridge and the 8,868-foot summit, typically another 3-5 hours. Any overnight stay requires a free North Cascades National Park backcountry permit — Eldorado is managed as a high-use cross-country zone with advance reservations available on Recreation.gov plus a limited walk-up allocation issued in person at the Marblemount Wilderness Information Center. IMPORTANT — this route is NOT on Eldorado Peak itself, despite being filed under the same area: from the Eldorado/Inspiration Glacier high camp (~7,600ft), continue traversing the Inspiration Glacier well past Eldorado's summit to Klawatti col; Tepeh Towers are passed along this same glacier traverse, and Austera Peak requires further travel across the Klawatti Glacier (including a short rappel from the col) beyond Klawatti Peak — meaningfully further and more committing than simply topping out on Eldorado.

routes.approach: From the Esmeralda/Lake Ingalls Trailhead at the end of FS-9737, hike the Ingalls Way Trail up switchbacks past several waterfalls to Longs Pass (6,200 ft) at about mile 2.5. For the Cascadian Couloir and South Headwall, descend the rocky climbers' trail on the far side of Longs Pass to Ingalls Creek, cross on a log bridge, and follow the climbers' path up the meadow into the base of the Cascadian Couloir (South Headwall lies further up-basin via the same drainage). For West Ridge, the North Ridge routes, and the South Face (Gorillas in the Mist / Gorillas Direct / King Kong), instead continue past Ingalls Lake toward Stuart Pass, then follow the climbers' trail onto the talus shoulder below Stuart's West Ridge and contour northwest to Goat Pass (7,600 ft) — the South Face wall with the Gorillas routes sits in the amphitheater roughly midway between Stuart Pass and Goat Pass, while West Ridge and North Ridge climbers continue over or along Goat Pass toward their respective start points. Camping is common near Goat Pass or down at Ingalls Creek depending on the objective. The North Ridge routes can also be reached from the opposite (Mountaineer Creek) side.

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road to FS-9737 (Esmeralda/Lake Ingalls Trailhead, aka Longs Pass TH)
road.status: gravel/dirt, washboarded and pothole-prone in its last miles; passable to most cars but high-clearance recommended for the final stretch
road.seasonalGate: no permanent gate, but subject to seasonal snow closure in winter/early spring; typically fully open by mid-summer
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum take SR-970 east ~11 miles, turn onto Teanaway River Road, then North Fork Teanaway Road; after the pavement ends, bear right onto FS-9737 and follow it to its end at the Esmeralda/Lake Ingalls Trailhead (also signed for Longs Pass Trail #1229).

routes.approach: From the Lake Stuart Trailhead, hike the Lake Stuart-Colchuck Lake Trail (#1599); at about 2.2 miles a fork splits toward Colchuck Lake (left) and Lake Stuart (right) — leave the maintained trail near the meadow around 4,500 ft and head southwest, following the south fork of Mountaineer Creek upstream. After a couple of miles, climb a steep talus/boulder slope into the hanging valley beneath Sherpa Peak and the Sherpa/Stuart Glaciers. Sherpa Glacier climbers ascend directly from this basin; Stuart Glacier Couloir climbers continue up into the couloir on Stuart's north side from the same basin. This is the standard approach for a compact two-day outing on these routes, though it can alternatively be reached from the south via Goat Pass by traversing across the upper Stuart Glacier.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Eightmile Road (FR-7600) to FR-7601 (Stuart Lake/Eightmile Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FR-7600/FR-7601 are steep, narrow, and washboarded gravel — high-clearance and ideally 4x4 recommended, especially later in the season
road.seasonalGate: FR-7601 is gated and closed to vehicles in winter, roughly November through May; open by early-mid summer
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Creek Road about 8.5 miles, turn left onto Eightmile Road (FR-7600), stay right onto FR-7601, and follow it about 4 miles (passing the Eightmile Trailhead) to the Stuart Lake/Lake Stuart Trailhead at ~3,600 ft.

routes.approach: From the Esmeralda/Lake Ingalls Trailhead at the end of FS-9737, hike the Ingalls Way Trail up switchbacks past several waterfalls to Longs Pass (6,200 ft) at about mile 2.5. For the Cascadian Couloir and South Headwall, descend the rocky climbers' trail on the far side of Longs Pass to Ingalls Creek, cross on a log bridge, and follow the climbers' path up the meadow into the base of the Cascadian Couloir (South Headwall lies further up-basin via the same drainage). For West Ridge, the North Ridge routes, and the South Face (Gorillas in the Mist / Gorillas Direct / King Kong), instead continue past Ingalls Lake toward Stuart Pass, then follow the climbers' trail onto the talus shoulder below Stuart's West Ridge and contour northwest to Goat Pass (7,600 ft) — the South Face wall with the Gorillas routes sits in the amphitheater roughly midway between Stuart Pass and Goat Pass, while West Ridge and North Ridge climbers continue over or along Goat Pass toward their respective start points. Camping is common near Goat Pass or down at Ingalls Creek depending on the objective. The North Ridge routes can also be reached from the opposite (Mountaineer Creek) side.

road.name: North Fork Teanaway Road to FS-9737 (Esmeralda/Lake Ingalls Trailhead, aka Longs Pass TH)
road.status: gravel/dirt, washboarded and pothole-prone in its last miles; passable to most cars but high-clearance recommended for the final stretch
road.seasonalGate: no permanent gate, but subject to seasonal snow closure in winter/early spring; typically fully open by mid-summer
road.driveNote: From Cle Elum take SR-970 east ~11 miles, turn onto Teanaway River Road, then North Fork Teanaway Road; after the pavement ends, bear right onto FS-9737 and follow it to its end at the Esmeralda/Lake Ingalls Trailhead (also signed for Longs Pass Trail #1229).

routes.approach: From the Lake Stuart Trailhead, hike the Lake Stuart-Colchuck Lake Trail (#1599); at about 2.2 miles a fork splits toward Colchuck Lake (left) and Lake Stuart (right) — leave the maintained trail near the meadow around 4,500 ft and head southwest, following the south fork of Mountaineer Creek upstream. After a couple of miles, climb a steep talus/boulder slope into the hanging valley beneath Sherpa Peak and the Sherpa/Stuart Glaciers. Sherpa Glacier climbers ascend directly from this basin; Stuart Glacier Couloir climbers continue up into the couloir on Stuart's north side from the same basin. This is the standard approach for a compact two-day outing on these routes, though it can alternatively be reached from the south via Goat Pass by traversing across the upper Stuart Glacier.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Eightmile Road (FR-7600) to FR-7601 (Stuart Lake/Eightmile Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FR-7600/FR-7601 are steep, narrow, and washboarded gravel — high-clearance and ideally 4x4 recommended, especially later in the season
road.seasonalGate: FR-7601 is gated and closed to vehicles in winter, roughly November through May; open by early-mid summer
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, drive Icicle Creek Road about 8.5 miles, turn left onto Eightmile Road (FR-7600), stay right onto FR-7601, and follow it about 4 miles (passing the Eightmile Trailhead) to the Stuart Lake/Lake Stuart Trailhead at ~3,600 ft.

routes.approach: The drive itself is easy — paved the whole way from Winthrop up the Chewuch River valley to the Andrews Creek Trailhead, about 28 miles total — but the climb to Cathedral Peak is one of the longest wilderness approaches in the state. From the trailhead, the Andrews Creek Trail (#504) climbs roughly 12 miles to 6,680+ ft Andrews Pass, then descends into the Spanish Creek drainage before a side trail climbs to Upper Cathedral Lakes below the peak's southwest side, putting the one-way approach at around 18 miles with significant elevation gain and loss along the way. Most parties treat this as a 2-4 day backpack (roughly 40+ miles round trip), camping at or near Cathedral Lakes before making a summit push. A free self-issue wilderness permit is required at the trailhead, along with a trailhead parking pass; the first many miles are described by trip reports as a hot, rocky, buggy uphill slog with little shade, so this is genuinely a remote, multi-day alpine objective rather than a day-approach crag. For this specific route: The Monk is a distinct, smaller detached tower/buttress leaning against Cathedral Peak's south/east flank, not part of the main South Face wall. From Cathedral Lakes camp, follow the same talus slope toward Cathedral Pass but hike up into the deep gully separating Cathedral Peak from The Monk, then start on The Monk's own faces (right side of the gully); most Monk routes descend via rappels down the NE gully.

road.name: Chewuch River Road (Forest Service Road 51), reached via West Chewuch Road from Winthrop
road.status: paved the entire way to the Andrews Creek Trailhead — no high-clearance or 4WD needed in summer season
road.seasonalGate: closed by snow in winter/spring; typically not reliably open until late spring/early summer, check current Okanogan-Wenatchee NF conditions before a trip
road.driveNote: From Winthrop, take WA-20 and turn onto West Chewuch Road (County Rd 1213); after about 6 miles it becomes Forest Service Road 51 (Chewuch River Road); continue roughly 22 more miles on pavement to its end at the Andrews Creek Trailhead.

For this specific route: from Cathedral Lakes camp, follow the trail toward Cathedral Pass and climb scree/talus to the base of the peak's main South Face; SE Buttress starts on the left wall just after entering the deep gully that separates Cathedral Peak from The Monk, while NE Ridge is gained by continuing up and around toward the ridgeline running north.

routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive west on US-2 to the edge of town, turn onto Icicle Creek Road, go about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto Forest Service Road 7601 and follow its rough, high-clearance-recommended dirt surface roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead. From there, the on-foot approach follows Trail #1599 for about 2.5 miles to a junction, then climbs via switchbacks roughly another 1.5 miles to Colchuck Lake (about 4 miles and ~2,200 ft of gain one-way, at 5,570 ft elevation) — already a strenuous day-hike in its own right. From the lake, climbers continue to the base of Aasgard Pass and grind up its infamous, largely unmaintained, boulder-and-scree slope, gaining roughly 2,000-2,300 ft in under a mile to the 7,800 ft pass, before dropping into the Core Enchantment Zone where Prusik Peak's iconic spire (near Gnome Tarn) comes into view. This is one of the most demanding standard approaches in the Cascades and is commonly done as a very long day or split over two days. Overnight camping anywhere in the Enchantment Permit Area (including the Core Zone) requires a highly competitive lottery permit via Recreation.gov, with applications typically open in a roughly two-week window in mid-to-late February for the May 15-October 31 permit season. Day hiking/climbing without camping does not require the lottery and instead only needs a free, self-issued day-use permit obtained at the trailhead.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Service Road 7601 (Stuart Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FS Road 7601 is unpaved, rough, and often washboarded/potholed — high-clearance vehicles recommended
road.seasonalGate: FS 7601 is gated and closed in winter, typically from around November through May depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow US-2 to the west edge of town and turn onto Icicle Creek Road (signed for the Leavenworth National Fish Hatchery); drive about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto FS Road 7601 and follow the rough dirt road roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

Overnight camping anywhere in the Enchantment Permit Area (including the Core Zone) requires a highly competitive lottery permit via Recreation.gov, with applications typically open in a roughly two-week window in mid-to-late February for the May 15-October 31 permit season. Day hiking/climbing without camping does not require the lottery and instead only needs a free, self-issued day-use permit obtained at the trailhead.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Service Road 7601 (Stuart Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FS Road 7601 is unpaved, rough, and often washboarded/potholed — high-clearance vehicles recommended
road.seasonalGate: FS 7601 is gated and closed in winter, typically from around November through May depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow US-2 to the west edge of town and turn onto Icicle Creek Road (signed for the Leavenworth National Fish Hatchery); drive about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto FS Road 7601 and follow the rough dirt road roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to the Arlington exit, then SR 530 east through Oso to Darrington, and turn onto Squire Creek Road at the west end of town, following it to the parking area where it's gated off by washout damage. From there the approach is long and rough: hike the old roadbed past a landslide crossing (~5 minutes in), reach the original road end around 30-40 minutes from the car, then follow a faint trail through a dry streambed and alder brush, ford Squire Creek, and pick through obscure forest track before finally breaking out into the open basin roughly 2 hours from the car. This is a known bushwhacky, unmaintained approach typical of Darrington-area crags, with route-finding (flagging, cairns) required in several spots.

road.name: Squire Creek Road (Forest Service Road 2040)
road.status: gravel/dirt, drivable to a blocked parking area but not beyond — a longstanding washout (dating to a 2002 landslide) has permanently cut off the upper roadbed to vehicles
road.driveNote: From I-5 take the Arlington exit and follow SR 530 east about 30 miles to Darrington; pick up Squire Creek Road at the west end of the old downtown and follow it to the parking area where the road is blocked by washout damage.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive east on I-90 to Snoqualmie Pass and take Exit 54, crossing to the north side of the freeway before turning onto FR 4832, then following it onto FR 4934 to the small trailhead pullout. On foot, cross the road and hike up a spur road for about 15 minutes to a flat bench, then pick up a cairned trail heading north into the forest to a rocky area, staying on the main climber's trail through a short, steeper forest section to reach the crag — roughly 25-30 minutes total from the car. A Northwest Forest Pass is generally expected for parking at national forest trailheads in this corridor.

road.name: Forest Roads 4832 and 4934 (off I-90 Exit 54, Gold Creek area)
road.status: gravel, generally passable to passenger cars in summer with some potholes; not maintained for winter travel
road.driveNote: Take I-90 Exit 54 (Gold Creek/Hyak area), head north over the freeway, then turn right onto FR 4832 paralleling I-90 eastbound; after about 4 miles bear left onto FR 4934 at a signed fork and continue roughly 3.25 miles to a small pullout parking area on the right.

routes.approach: The Dikes sit in the Umatilla National Forest's Walla Walla Ranger District, in the East Fork Touchet River valley near Ski Bluewood, southeast Washington — not near the Snake River. Access is via North Touchet Road out of Dayton, WA, paved for roughly the first 15-16 miles before becoming gravel Forest Service Road 64 at the forest boundary; the road is passable roughly April-November and can hold snow into early summer. Approaches are very short and roadside: the south Megadike is reached by parking near a bridge 1.2 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead and scrambling directly up the steep hillside above the bridge abutment, while Weeping Wall and Joe's Rock are similar short pullout-to-cliff scrambles at 1.1 and 2.0 miles past the same trailhead respectively. The area is lightly visited and faint-trailed, and the basalt is notably loose/fractured, requiring routine loose-rock cleaning. This route is on Megadike South: drive 1.2 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead and park on either side of the road just before the bridge over the river. Scramble up the steep hillside on the NW side of the road above the bridge abutment, then continue uphill along the dike to a passable gully.

road.name: North Touchet Road → Forest Service Road 64 (Kendall Skyline Road)
road.status: Paved county road (North Touchet Road) for the first ~15-16 miles from Dayton; becomes gravel FS Road 64 at the national forest boundary near Ski Bluewood — well-graded gravel initially, narrowing to a rougher single-lane, sometimes rutted road further in. High-clearance is advisable.
road.seasonalGate: No formal locked gate confirmed, but the road/trailhead is only reliably open roughly April through November per the Forest Service; snow can linger into early summer and the route is not maintained/plowed in winter.
road.driveNote: From Dayton, WA, head south on S 4th St (becomes North Touchet Road) toward the Ski Bluewood sign; continue ~16-17 miles to the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead, then continue another 1.1-2.0 miles past it to roadside pullouts serving the different sub-areas.

routes.approach: The Dikes sit in the Umatilla National Forest's Walla Walla Ranger District, in the East Fork Touchet River valley near Ski Bluewood, southeast Washington — not near the Snake River. Access is via North Touchet Road out of Dayton, WA, paved for roughly the first 15-16 miles before becoming gravel Forest Service Road 64 at the forest boundary; the road is passable roughly April-November and can hold snow into early summer. Approaches are very short and roadside: the south Megadike is reached by parking near a bridge 1.2 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead and scrambling directly up the steep hillside above the bridge abutment, while Weeping Wall and Joe's Rock are similar short pullout-to-cliff scrambles at 1.1 and 2.0 miles past the same trailhead respectively. The area is lightly visited and faint-trailed, and the basalt is notably loose/fractured, requiring routine loose-rock cleaning. This route is on Megadike North: park 1.1 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead, where a dirt road intersects the paved road on the left (SE side), or continue to 1.2 miles and use the large pull-off. Cross the main road and pick up the approach trail between the two parking spots, marked by a short steep drainage topped with a downed tree.

Access is via North Touchet Road out of Dayton, WA, paved for roughly the first 15-16 miles before becoming gravel Forest Service Road 64 at the forest boundary; the road is passable roughly April-November and can hold snow into early summer. Approaches are very short and roadside: the south Megadike is reached by parking near a bridge 1.2 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead and scrambling directly up the steep hillside above the bridge abutment, while Weeping Wall and Joe's Rock are similar short pullout-to-cliff scrambles at 1.1 and 2.0 miles past the same trailhead respectively. The area is lightly visited and faint-trailed, and the basalt is notably loose/fractured, requiring routine loose-rock cleaning. This route is on the Minidike: park 1.5 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead on the left (SE) side of the road, just after crossing a bridge over the river. Cross the road, scramble down the bridge abutment, and follow a trail downstream on the NW side.

road.name: North Touchet Road → Forest Service Road 64 (Kendall Skyline Road)
road.status: Paved county road (North Touchet Road) for the first ~15-16 miles from Dayton; becomes gravel FS Road 64 at the national forest boundary near Ski Bluewood — well-graded gravel initially, narrowing to a rougher single-lane, sometimes rutted road further in. High-clearance is advisable.
road.seasonalGate: No formal locked gate confirmed, but the road/trailhead is only reliably open roughly April through November per the Forest Service; snow can linger into early summer and the route is not maintained/plowed in winter.

This route is on Megadike North: park 1.1 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead, where a dirt road intersects the paved road on the left (SE side), or continue to 1.2 miles and use the large pull-off. Cross the main road and pick up the approach trail between the two parking spots, marked by a short steep drainage topped with a downed tree.

This route is on Megadike South: drive 1.2 miles past the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead and park on either side of the road just before the bridge over the river. Scramble up the steep hillside on the NW side of the road above the bridge abutment, then continue uphill along the dike to a passable gully.

This route is on The Castle: from the Middle Point Ridge Trailhead, continue about 1.2 miles further down the road (0.6 miles past where The Castle first becomes visible on the ridge) and park on the right (east) side where a dirt road from the Spangler Creek valley intersects the main road. Climb a long, very steep hillside to the base of the pinnacle.

routes.approach: The standard climbers' access is an unmarked pullout on the north side of SR-20 roughly 1.5 miles west of the Washington Pass overlook (lower in elevation and about 2 miles further west than the Blue Lake trailhead) — this is a separate, shorter boot path used specifically for the South Buttress/Southeast Buttress climbs, distinct from the longer, maintained Cutthroat Lake Trail (FS Trail #483) that starts near Lone Fir Campground on the east side of the pass. From the pullout, a climber's trail drops through the woods, crosses Cutthroat Creek (losing roughly 150 ft that must be regained on the return), and continues up to the large meadow and boulder field below the south buttress, about 1.5-2 hours one-way. From there, the leftmost of the gullies descending from the buttress leads to the base of the route, with roping up typically starting a few hundred feet below the ridge crest. For this specific route: shared trail crosses the creek into the large meadow, climbs into the boulder field below the south buttress, then bears right to the leftmost of the gullies coming down from the buttress/ridge.

road.name: SR-20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: Paved 2-lane highway; open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Washington Pass closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary by year and are set by WSDOT); gated near Ross Dam (MP ~134) and Early Winters/Silver Star (MP ~171-178) depending on conditions
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass, ~170-180 mi / 3-3.5 hrs. From Winthrop/Mazama: ~20-25 min west on SR-20.

From there, the leftmost of the gullies descending from the buttress leads to the base of the route, with roping up typically starting a few hundred feet below the ridge crest. For this specific route: after the shared creek crossing, continue up meadows into the bowl southwest of the summit (not the south-buttress boulder field) to a saddle just west of the West Ridge, then traverse east about 100 ft along the ridge's south face to a shallow gully.

routes.approach: The Temple (Kangaroo Temple, on Kangaroo Ridge) is reached from the large pullout at the hairpin turns on SR-20 just east of Washington Pass — the same parking area used for the neighboring Hairpin Crags/Spire Gully climbs. From the pullout, a climber's trail heads south across a boulder field, through forest and more talus (faint, intermittently cairned) up to Kangaroo Pass, about 1 hour. From the pass, an intermittent trail traverses to a gully between Kangaroo Temple and Little Finger; ascending or descending that gully reaches the base of the North Face, Northwest Face, or Southwest Face routes, roughly another hour. Total trailhead-to-summit time runs about 7-8 hours round trip. For this specific route: boulder field to Kangaroo Pass, traverse left to the gully between Kangaroo Temple and Little Finger, then ascend toward the notch. Northwest Face breaks off onto a climber's trail about 200 ft below the notch instead of continuing into the boulder-choked gully.

IMPORTANT — this route uses a genuinely different approach than Temple's North/Northwest Face routes: park at the same hairpin pullout but hike southeast on the right side of the creek on a trail that improves past several slide/boulder areas, never crossing Kangaroo Pass. Longer and steeper, and the face isn't visible from the highway.

routes.approach: Verification strongly suggests this 'Mamie Peak' entry is mislabeled or miscategorized as a Washington Pass area — its coordinates correspond to the Hannegan Pass/Ruth Creek Valley area near Mt. Baker, sometimes referenced in trip reports as 'Ellation Crags,' a minor, lightly-developed granite cragging/multipitch area. The general access is from I-5 Exit 255, east on SR-542 (Mt. Baker Highway) about 46 miles, then a left onto Forest Road 32 (Hannegan Pass Road) for about 4 miles to the Hannegan Pass Trailhead. Beyond that trailhead, published approach details for the crag itself are sparse and inconsistent across the few available trip reports, so the exact on-foot route and distance could not be confidently verified and should be treated as unconfirmed rather than stated as precise fact.

road.name: SR-542 (Mt. Baker Highway) + Forest Road 32
road.status: Paved highway (SR-542) to gravel Forest Service road (FR-32); seasonal snow access varies by year
road.driveNote: This area is NOT part of the Washington Pass cluster despite shared North Cascades categorization — its coordinates place it near Hannegan Pass/Ruth Creek in the Mt. Baker area, roughly 90 miles northwest of Washington Pass, reached via a completely different highway (SR-542, not SR-20).

routes.approach: Spire Gully right (Alpenkuhl), part of the Hairpin Crags, is reached from the same large pullout at the hairpin turns on SR-20 near Washington Pass used for Kangaroo Temple. Looking up toward South Early Winter Spire from the pullout, Spire Gully is the obvious gully just below and left; Alpenkuhl is its sunnier, south-facing right side (the left side is known as Tiger Wall). The approach is one of the shortest at Washington Pass — a direct walk/scramble of roughly 10-15 minutes straight up from the highway, snow-covered in early season and a worn boot path later in summer.

road.seasonalGate: Washington Pass closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary by year); gated near Ross Dam (MP ~134) and Early Winters/Silver Star (MP ~171-178) depending on conditions
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass, ~170-180 mi / 3-3.5 hrs. From Winthrop/Mazama: ~20-25 min west on SR-20.

routes.approach: Spire Gully right (Alpenkuhl), part of the Hairpin Crags, is reached from the same large pullout at the hairpin turns on SR-20 near Washington Pass used for Kangaroo Temple. Looking up toward South Early Winter Spire from the pullout, Spire Gully is the obvious gully just below and left; Alpenkuhl is its sunnier, south-facing right side (the left side is known as Tiger Wall). The approach is one of the shortest at Washington Pass — a direct walk/scramble of roughly 10-15 minutes straight up from the highway, snow-covered in early season and a worn boot path later in summer.

routes.approach: M&M Wall, also known as Supercave Wall for the tennis-court-sized cave crossing the cliff at mid-height, is one of the largest roadside walls at Washington Pass. Park at a pullout near milepost 166 on SR-20, about 3 miles east of the hairpin turns (and a similar distance from the Washington Pass summit). From the highway, head directly uphill (north) toward the talus at the base of the Supercave gully, following it up and briefly left before rejoining the gully; it takes roughly 1 hour to reach the lower ledge and about 1 hour 10 minutes to reach the upper ledge and base of the longer routes.

routes.approach: From Granite Falls, drive the paved Mountain Loop Highway east roughly 27-29 miles (well short of the unpaved stretch that begins near Barlow Pass), then turn right onto Sunrise Mine Road (Forest Road 4065) and follow this rough gravel road about 2.2-2.5 miles to its end at the Sunrise Mine Trailhead (~2,300 ft). On foot, Sunrise Mine Trail #707 climbs steeply through forest with several unbridged creek crossings for about 2.7 miles to Headlee Pass (~4,700 ft), then continues past Vesper Lake to the East Ridge scramble route on Vesper's south side. The full round trip to the 6,221-ft summit runs roughly 9-10 miles with around 4,000 feet of elevation gain, making it one of the steeper, more strenuous popular scrambles along the Mountain Loop corridor.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065)
road.status: Mountain Loop Highway is paved the entire way from Granite Falls to the Sunrise Mine Road turnoff; Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065) itself is a narrow, rough single-lane gravel road for its final ~2.2-2.5 miles to the trailhead.
road.driveNote: A Northwest Forest Pass is required at the trailhead, which has vault toilets but no other services; the gravel spur is passable to most passenger vehicles in dry conditions but can be rutted or snow-covered into early summer.

routes.approach: Morning Star Peak is reached via the same Sunrise Mine Trailhead as Vesper Peak, off Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065) from the paved Mountain Loop Highway. Hikers follow Sunrise Mine Trail #707 about 2.5 miles, gaining roughly 2,700 feet, to a major switchback near 4,300 feet just below Headlee Pass. From there the standard south ridge route leaves the trail and traverses northeast across talus and heather toward the Mile High Buttress before climbing a gully/ledge system to the summit ridge, rather than continuing over Headlee Pass itself. This off-trail scramble/easy alpine route can hold steep snow and ice near the pass in early season and heavy brush in summer, so it is generally treated as a more involved undertaking than the adjacent Vesper Peak trail.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Sunrise Mine Road (FR 4065)
road.status: Same access as Vesper Peak: Mountain Loop Highway is paved to the Sunrise Mine Road turnoff, then FR 4065 is a rough single-lane gravel road for its final ~2.2-2.5 miles to the shared trailhead.
road.driveNote: Shares the Sunrise Mine Trailhead with Vesper Peak, so the same Northwest Forest Pass and road-condition notes apply; no separate trailhead or access road exists for Morning Star.

routes.approach: Access is via the Mountain Loop Highway (paved) to Bedal, then onto gravel Forest Road 49 (Sloan Creek Road) to the North Fork Sauk River Trailhead (Trail #649), which is the standard start for the Corkscrew Route rather than a separate 'Sloan Creek Trailhead.' From the trailhead the route follows an old roadbed/trail about half a mile to a ford of the North Fork Sauk River, which can be hazardous and is safest at low water in late summer/early fall; beyond the ford the trail climbs steeply through forest into the Glacier Peak Wilderness, crossing Cougar Creek near a waterfall roughly two miles past the ford. From there the route leaves maintained trail, crossing an avalanche-scoured slope with obliterated tread before climbing cross-country into meadows and a basin at the base of Sloan Peak's southeast side, where climbers typically camp on a saddle (~5,300 ft) or a ridge above the glacier (~5,800 ft). Overall the approach to camp is commonly reported as a long day (roughly 4-5 hours one-way), with total round-trip stats for the climb in the range of about 10-12 miles and 5,900-6,000+ feet of cumulative elevation gain to the ~7,835-foot summit via the Corkscrew's distinctive rock shelf between cliff bands.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Sloan Creek Road (Forest Road 49)
road.status: paved on the Mountain Loop Highway; gravel, narrow single-lane, high-clearance recommended on the final ~6.6-7.5 miles of FR 49 to the trailhead
road.seasonalGate: not gated as a rule, but like most Glacier Peak Wilderness approach roads it can be snow-covered, washed out, or otherwise impassable into early summer; check current Darrington Ranger District/MBS National Forest alerts before driving
road.driveNote: From Darrington, drive south on the Mountain Loop Highway roughly 16-20 miles (or from the Verlot side, about 26.5 miles east from the Verlot Public Service Center) to the signed Sloan Creek Road (FR 49) junction near Bedal Campground, then follow FR 49 about 6.6-7.5 miles of rough gravel road to the North Fork Sauk River Trailhead.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington, then WA-20 east through Marblemount, and turn onto Cascade River Road, following its paved-then-gravel surface about 21.7 miles to a small pullout at 3,200 ft that marks the unmaintained Boston Basin Trailhead (a different, lower stop than the Eldorado trailhead a mile or so up-road, and just before the road's end at Cascade Pass). From there a steep, unmaintained climbers' path follows the old Diamond Mine roadbed briefly before climbing hard, crossing several streams, and breaking above treeline (~5,300 ft) to reach the popular Boston Basin high camp around 6,000 ft — roughly 3-4 miles one-way with about 3,000-3,300 ft of elevation gain. From camp, West Ridge climbers continue cross-country over the glacier to a couloir that gains the ridge crest. Because Boston Basin is a high-demand cross-country camping zone in North Cascades National Park, overnight parties need a wilderness permit; a portion of peak-season (mid-May to mid-October) slots can be reserved in advance via Recreation.gov's early-access lottery, with the remainder issued as limited first-come, first-served walk-up permits. For this specific route: still from Boston Basin high camp, but diverges from the West Ridge line — ascend north across glacier/firn toward the South Face, then bear northeast to a distinct, narrow snow couloir/gully leading to an upper shoulder and bivy spot below the East Ridge.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Paved for the first stretch out of Marblemount, then becomes gravel/washboard with potholes for the final miles; passable to most cars in good conditions but a high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially near road's end.
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed each winter (often near Eldorado Creek, roughly MP 20); NPS/Forest Service typically reopen it in late spring (around late May-June) once snow and storm damage are cleared, so early-season access can be blocked or delayed by washouts.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 north to Burlington (exit ~230), then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles to a small pullout at ~3,200 ft marking the unsigned Boston Basin Trailhead, just short of the road's end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~23 miles).

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington, then WA-20 east through Marblemount, and turn onto Cascade River Road, following its paved-then-gravel surface about 21.7 miles to a small pullout at 3,200 ft that marks the unmaintained Boston Basin Trailhead (a different, lower stop than the Eldorado trailhead a mile or so up-road, and just before the road's end at Cascade Pass). From there a steep, unmaintained climbers' path follows the old Diamond Mine roadbed briefly before climbing hard, crossing several streams, and breaking above treeline (~5,300 ft) to reach the popular Boston Basin high camp around 6,000 ft — roughly 3-4 miles one-way with about 3,000-3,300 ft of elevation gain. From camp, West Ridge climbers continue cross-country over the glacier to a couloir that gains the ridge crest. Because Boston Basin is a high-demand cross-country camping zone in North Cascades National Park, overnight parties need a wilderness permit; a portion of peak-season (mid-May to mid-October) slots can be reserved in advance via Recreation.gov's early-access lottery, with the remainder issued as limited first-come, first-served walk-up permits. IMPORTANT — this route also does not stay in Boston Basin proper: either a near-complete circumnavigation of the peak via the Quien Sabe, Boston, and Forbidden Glaciers, or climb the West Ridge couloir to a high bivy near the West Ridge notch, then make a series of rappels down steep snow/rock onto the remote Forbidden Glacier below — a different final glacier than both the West Ridge and North Ridge approaches.

IMPORTANT — this route genuinely leaves Boston Basin: ascend to the toe of the Quien Sabe Glacier and climb it to Sharkfin Col (~7,720 ft), then rappel about 150 ft down a loose gully onto the Boston Glacier — the remote Boston Glacier side, as opposed to the popular Boston Basin side. Traverse north across the Boston Glacier (crossing crevasses) to reach the base of the North Ridge/face. Typically a multi-day approach with a bivouac.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Paved for the first stretch out of Marblemount, then becomes gravel/washboard with potholes for the final miles; passable to most cars in good conditions but a high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially near road's end.
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed each winter (often near Eldorado Creek, roughly MP 20); NPS/Forest Service typically reopen it in late spring (around late May-June) once snow and storm damage are cleared, so early-season access can be blocked or delayed by washouts.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 north to Burlington (exit ~230), then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles to a small pullout at ~3,200 ft marking the unsigned Boston Basin Trailhead, just short of the road's end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~23 miles).

routes.approach: Amphitheatre Mountain has no trailhead of its own — it's reached by the same long approach used for neighboring Cathedral Peak. From Winthrop, drive north on Chewuch River Road (Forest Road 51), paved for about 15 miles then gravel, roughly 23.5 miles to the Andrews Creek Trailhead. From there it's a genuine multi-day wilderness backpack: the Andrews Creek Trail climbs about 12 miles to Andrews Pass (6,680+ ft), then the route drops into the Spanish Creek drainage before climbing again to Cathedral Lakes, reaching Upper Cathedral Lake at the north foot of Amphitheatre's west summit after roughly 18 miles and well over 4,000 ft of cumulative gain one-way. An alternative western approach via Loomis and Toats Coulee/Forest Road 39 reaches the same basin after a comparably long (~20-mile) walk-in. Either way this is a serious, remote Pasayten Wilderness objective best planned as a 3-5 day trip and typically only feasible from July through September.

road.name: Chewuch River Road (Forest Road 51) from Winthrop
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 15 miles to Chewuch Campground, then becomes a gravel Forest Service road for the remainder; only reliably snow-free in summer
road.seasonalGate: Unplowed/snow-closed beyond Chewuch Campground outside summer; exact gate dates vary by year and are best confirmed with the Methow Valley Ranger District
road.driveNote: About 23.5 miles north of Winthrop to the Andrews Creek Trailhead (3,050 ft); standard clearance is usually adequate in dry summer conditions but the road is thinly documented, so check current status before driving in

routes.approach: Take US-2 east from Seattle/Everett over Stevens Pass, then drop onto the old Stevens Pass Highway (now Tye Rd), which heads toward the Iron Horse Trailhead and the Wellington townsite; park in the pullout just uphill of a double hairpin turn roughly 1.4 miles from US-2. From there, the on-foot approach follows only faint boot-path traces through open old-growth forest, generally trending uphill and left across a drainage to reach the base of the crag. Expect about 1,000 feet of elevation gain and 45-60 minutes of hiking, with route-finding skills helpful since the tread is intermittent.

road.name: US-2 (Stevens Pass Highway) to Old Cascade Highway (Tye Rd) pullout
road.status: US-2 is a paved, year-round state highway; the old highway spur (Tye Rd) toward Wellington/Iron Horse Trailhead is a paved-to-gravel old roadbed passable by any car in dry conditions
road.seasonalGate: Not formally gated, but the old highway spur can be snow-covered or hard to find in winter/early spring since it isn't plowed like US-2 itself
road.driveNote: From Seattle/Everett, take US-2 east to Stevens Pass, then continue on the old Stevens Pass Highway (Tye Rd) downhill toward the Iron Horse Trailhead and the historic Wellington townsite; pull off just uphill of a double hairpin turn, about 1.4 miles off US-2.

routes.approach: Access is via Port Angeles up the paved Hurricane Ridge Road (an NPS entrance fee/pass is required), then onto the unpaved, narrow Obstruction Point Road for about 2 miles to a point where the road runs almost directly beneath the formation. Steeple Rock is a roughly 200-foot spire close enough to the road that the walk-up is short, commonly described as around 20 minutes or less depending on which side is approached. Documentation on this crag is thin compared to other Washington areas — it sees light traffic and exact tread/mileage for the final scramble to the base isn't well pinned down in available sources. Because Obstruction Point Road is seasonal and weather-dependent, this approach is realistically only viable roughly mid-July through early October. For this specific route: continue to the northernmost crack on Steeple Rock's west face — a separate scramble from the north-ridgeline or south-side routes.

road.name: Hurricane Ridge Road to Obstruction Point Road, Olympic National Park
road.status: Hurricane Ridge Road is paved; Obstruction Point Road beyond it is a narrow, unpaved dirt/gravel mountain road with steep drop-offs and washboard sections, generally driveable in a standard car but with white-knuckle passing spots
road.seasonalGate: Obstruction Point Road is gated/closed most of the year, typically open only mid-July through early October (weather and damage permitting); Hurricane Ridge Road itself is limited to Friday-Sunday during winter operations (late November-March)
road.driveNote: From Port Angeles, take US-101 to the park entrance and Hurricane Ridge Road, follow it roughly 17 miles as it climbs to the Obstruction Point Road junction on the left, then continue on Obstruction Point Road about 2 miles to where the road passes beneath Steeple Rock.

routes.approach: Access is via Port Angeles up the paved Hurricane Ridge Road (an NPS entrance fee/pass is required), then onto the unpaved, narrow Obstruction Point Road for about 2 miles to a point where the road runs almost directly beneath the formation. For this specific route: head to the north ridgeline via scree/heather along the west slope to an obvious notch, then contour east on a faint trail.

routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 52, follow Alpental Road up to the ski area and park along Erste Strasse near the base chalets, taking care to respect posted no-parking signs on the private-property sections of the road. For the south-face/rock routes, the crag rises directly above the parking area with essentially no approach hike required. For routes on other aspects of the mountain, use the Commonwealth Basin/PCT North Trailhead a short distance up the highway instead, following the Pacific Crest Trail into Commonwealth Basin before cutting up toward the peak. Because of the short, direct access, Guye Peak is climbed both summer and winter, though rock routes are primarily a snow-free-season (summer-fall) objective.

road.name: Alpental Road (Road 9041) via I-90 Exit 52, Snoqualmie Pass
road.status: Paved and open year-round, maintained for Alpental ski area access
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-90 east about 30-45 minutes to Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass/Alpental), then follow Alpental Road/Road 9041 toward the ski area, parking in pullouts along Erste Strasse near the base chalets, directly below Guye Peak's south face.

routes.approach: Dorado Needle sits on the same Eldorado Peak massif as the standard Main Peak route, reached via the same Eldorado trailhead on Cascade River Road (from Seattle via WA-20 to Marblemount, then Cascade River Road, paved then gravel, to mile 20). From the trailhead, cross the Cascade River, follow the steep climbers' boot path up through forest to the boulder field, cross Eldorado Creek, and continue into the alpine Eldorado Creek basin camp (roughly 5-6 hours from the car). From camp, continue onto the glacier (roped travel required) toward the Eldorado-Dorado Needle col, from which Dorado Needle's routes are accessed rather than continuing to Eldorado's main summit. The same North Cascades National Park backcountry permit requirement applies for any overnight stay. IMPORTANT — this is the most divergent of Dorado Needle's approaches: continue past both ridge cols to the Marble Creek-McAllister Pass (~7,600 ft) at the southwest edge of the glacier, then descend into Marble Creek cirque on the far (southwest) side of the peak — a different basin than the NW/East Ridge starts.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally; paved for the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then rough gravel/washboard with potholes for the remaining ~13 miles to the road's end at Cascade Pass Trailhead (mile 23.1). The Eldorado parking area/trailhead is at roughly mile 20.
road.seasonalGate: No permanent gate, but the road is not maintained through winter/spring snowpack and typically doesn't fully open to its end until late June or early July.
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 north to the WA-20 exit near Burlington, then east on WA-20 through Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, and Rockport to Marblemount. At Marblemount, turn right (south) onto Cascade River Road and continue about 20 miles to the Eldorado parking area — the last ~10 miles are gravel.

For this specific route: from the McAllister Glacier, stop at the Dorado Needle-Eldorado col (~7,600 ft) and ascend the glacier above it heading northwest to the base of the gendarmes on the NW ridge.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Open seasonally; paved for the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then rough gravel/washboard with potholes for the remaining ~13 miles to the road's end at Cascade Pass Trailhead (mile 23.1). The Eldorado parking area/trailhead is at roughly mile 20.
road.seasonalGate: No permanent gate, but the road is not maintained through winter/spring snowpack and typically doesn't fully open to its end until late June or early July.
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 north to the WA-20 exit near Burlington, then east on WA-20 through Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, and Rockport to Marblemount. At Marblemount, turn right (south) onto Cascade River Road and continue about 20 miles to the Eldorado parking area — the last ~10 miles are gravel.

routes.approach: Dorado Needle sits on the same Eldorado Peak massif as the standard Main Peak route, reached via the same Eldorado trailhead on Cascade River Road (from Seattle via WA-20 to Marblemount, then Cascade River Road, paved then gravel, to mile 20). From the trailhead, cross the Cascade River, follow the steep climbers' boot path up through forest to the boulder field, cross Eldorado Creek, and continue into the alpine Eldorado Creek basin camp (roughly 5-6 hours from the car). From camp, continue onto the glacier (roped travel required) toward the Eldorado-Dorado Needle col, from which Dorado Needle's routes are accessed rather than continuing to Eldorado's main summit. The same North Cascades National Park backcountry permit requirement applies for any overnight stay. For this specific route: continue past the NW Ridge's col to a different col between Pt. 8386 and Dorado Needle (~7,600 ft), on the opposite (east) side of the peak from the NW Ridge start.

routes.approach: South Face is another wall within the same Squire Creek Walls complex as Waterfall Basin, reached via the same Darrington-area approach: I-5 to the Arlington exit, SR 530 east through Oso to Darrington, then Squire Creek Road to the washout-blocked parking area. From there, follow the same rough, unmaintained approach — the old roadbed past a landslide crossing, a faint trail through streambed and alder brush, a ford of Squire Creek, and obscure forest track — before breaking into the open basin roughly 2 hours from the car; South Face is one of the walls encountered within that same basin complex, with route-finding (flagging, cairns) required in several spots.

road.name: Squire Creek Road (Forest Service Road 2040)
road.status: gravel/dirt, drivable to a blocked parking area but not beyond — a longstanding washout (dating to a 2002 landslide) has permanently cut off the upper roadbed to vehicles
road.driveNote: From I-5 take the Arlington exit and follow SR 530 east about 30 miles to Darrington; pick up Squire Creek Road at the west end of the old downtown and follow it to the parking area where the road is blocked by washout damage.

routes.approach: From the pullout, find a faint boot path descending loose talus/scree to the east into the trees, passing a trail register, then crossing Early Winters Creek on a log (~4,080 ft). The trail climbs the timbered rib north of Burgundy Creek, steeply gaining elevation to a basin/camp bench around 6,500 ft (roughly 2 hours from the car), then continues up to Burgundy Col (~7,720 ft, ~3 hours). Burgundy Spire is reached from the basin/col area with a short additional scramble; good camping and a late-season spring are near 6,400-6,500 ft.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: paved highway, seasonally closed
road.seasonalGate: Closed roughly mid/late November to mid/late April for avalanche danger; WSDOT's early-season gates are Ross Dam (MP134) and the Silver Star gate (MP171), later retreating to Newhalem (MP120) and Early Winters (MP178).
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass/Washington Pass, ~170-180 mi/3-3.5 hrs. Park at an unmarked pullout on the east side of the highway about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (near milepost 166, ~4,250-4,310 ft).

routes.approach: Two approaches exist. The common south approach (via Burgundy Col) follows the same boot path/trail as Burgundy Spire and the other Wine Spires: talus down to Early Winters Creek, up the timbered rib to a ~6,500 ft camp bench, then to Burgundy Col (~7,720 ft) before continuing onto the mountain (e.g. the Silver Star Glacier or NE ridge routes). A longer, less-used north approach ascends Silver Star Creek's east side for about 2 miles to a basin at ~4,800 ft, crosses west, and climbs toward Burgundy Col avoiding the headwall gullies; this route is brushier/scree-covered in late season but a good ski/snowshoe approach in spring with snow cover.

The standard south-side approach uses the same unmarked pullout ~4 miles east of Washington Pass (near milepost 166, ~4,250-4,310 ft) used for the Wine Spires; a separate, longer north-side approach starts from parking near Silver Star Creek further east on SR-20.

routes.approach: The Silver Horn is a subsidiary point on Silver Star Mountain's north ridge among the Wine Spires cluster, sharing the same approach as Burgundy Spire and Silver Star Mountain: talus down to Early Winters Creek, up the timbered rib to the ~6,500 ft basin/camp, then to Burgundy Col (~7,720 ft), from which it is reached by scrambling/traversing along the ridge. Specific route/approach detail for this exact sub-summit is thin in public trip reports beyond its shared basin access.

routes.approach: Pernod Spire is described in climbing references as one of the Wine Spires group (with Chianti and Chablis Spires), sitting near/right of Burgundy Col, and reached via the same climbers' trail as Burgundy Spire: talus down to Early Winters Creek, up the timbered rib to the ~6,500 ft camp bench, then to Burgundy Col (~7,720 ft) before traversing to the spire itself. For this specific route: from Burgundy Col (7,770 ft), traverse a short distance around the base of Pernod Spire to reach the south-facing or west-facing side of the formation before starting to climb.

routes.approach: Follow the shared Wine Spires trail: boot path down talus to Early Winters Creek, cross on a log, then climb steeply up the timbered rib north of Burgundy Creek to the ~6,500 ft basin/camp (roughly 2 hours from the car). Paisano Pinnacle's West Ridge route starts lower than Burgundy Spire's and is commonly linked with Burgundy Spire's North Face in one long car-to-car day; fresh water is reported from a spring near the camp bench.

routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead (~5,200 ft), follow the maintained Blue Lake Trail #314 about 1.5 miles to a well-worn climbers' path that branches off into the basin below the Liberty Bell Group (shared approach for Liberty Bell, Concord Tower, and Lexington Tower). Follow the climbers' trail into the basin, then scramble talus/slabs to the base of the tower; specific approach varies somewhat by which face/route is being climbed. For this specific route: from the Blue Lake Trailhead, the climber's trail (marked by a large cairn) leads into the basin on the west side of the Liberty Bell Group, then a short scramble to the base of the tower.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Blue Lake Trailhead
road.status: Paved highway to a maintained trailhead parking lot; Northwest Forest Pass required
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). Blue Lake Trailhead is on SR-20 about 1.5 mi west of Washington Pass. From Winthrop/Mazama, it's a short drive west on SR-20.

routes.approach: From the Blue Lake Trailhead (~5,200 ft), follow the maintained Blue Lake Trail #314 about 1.5 miles to a well-worn climbers' path that branches off into the basin below the Liberty Bell Group (shared approach for Liberty Bell, Concord Tower, and Lexington Tower). road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). Blue Lake Trailhead is on SR-20 about 1.5 mi west of Washington Pass. From Winthrop/Mazama, it's a short drive west on SR-20.

routes.approach: This route does NOT use the Blue Lake Trailhead, unlike Lexington Tower's North Face and Tooth and Claw. Park at the paved pullout on SR-20 near the Washington Pass Overlook turnoff (several hundred yards west of the hairpin turn, just east of a small pond). A separate climber's trail at the east end of the pond climbs about 30 minutes through timber to a boulder/scree field at the eastern base of the towers — the opposite side of the formation from North Face/Tooth and Claw.

road.name: SR-20 pullout near Washington Pass Overlook
road.status: Paved highway pullout, no formal trailhead facilities — distinct from the Blue Lake Trailhead used for this tower's other routes
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April
road.driveNote: From Seattle via SR-20 through Marblemount and Rainy Pass, or from Winthrop/Mazama a short drive west, park at the pullout near the Washington Pass Overlook turnoff — not the Blue Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: From the pullout, an unmarked but well-worn climbers' trail drops steeply from the west/southwest end of the turnout through loose dirt and rock into the trees, marked intermittently by cairns. The path crosses Early Winters Creek (on logs) before climbing into an open rocky drainage/talus below the formation; the final approach to the base involves loose 3rd/4th-class slabs, with sandy access ledges and a fixed rope on parts of the standard line.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) roadside pullout near Washington Pass
road.status: Paved highway shoulder pullout, no formal trailhead facilities
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). Park at a large pullout on SR-20 around milepost 165, roughly 2 miles east of Washington Pass, in the same general stretch of highway as the M&M Wall/Supercave approach. From Winthrop/Mazama, it's a short drive west on SR-20.

routes.approach: This is a distinct approach from the one used for Cutthroat Peak, which starts from an unmarked highway pullout about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass with no maintained trail. For Cutthroat Wall, start on the maintained Cutthroat Creek Trail #483 from the Cutthroat Trailhead kiosk. After a short distance on the main trail, leave it via a cairned path that cuts into the forest, then follow cairns and a climbers' path (roughly 1.5-2 miles, 45 min-2 hrs total) through brush and talus, trending toward the buttress, to reach the base of Cutthroat Wall.

road.name: SR-20 to Cutthroat Trailhead via Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400)
road.status: Paved highway to a maintained USFS trailhead with kiosk and paved/graded parking; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; parking is limited and fills quickly
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April; the trailhead itself is inaccessible whenever the highway is closed
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). From Winthrop, continue west on SR-20 about 28 miles (roughly 1.5 mi west of Lone Fir Campground), then turn right (north) onto Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400) and follow it about 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

routes.approach: This is a distinct approach from the one used for Cutthroat Peak, which starts from an unmarked highway pullout about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass with no maintained trail. road.status: Paved highway to a maintained USFS trailhead with kiosk and paved/graded parking; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; parking is limited and fills quickly
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April; the trailhead itself is inaccessible whenever the highway is closed
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). From Winthrop, continue west on SR-20 about 28 miles (roughly 1.5 mi west of Lone Fir Campground), then turn right (north) onto Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400) and follow it about 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

routes.approach: This is a distinct approach from the one used for Cutthroat Peak, which starts from an unmarked highway pullout about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass with no maintained trail. For Cutthroat Wall, start on the maintained Cutthroat Creek Trail #483 from the Cutthroat Trailhead kiosk. After a short distance on the main trail, leave it via a cairned path that cuts into the forest, then follow cairns and a climbers' path (roughly 1.5-2 miles, 45 min-2 hrs total) through brush and talus, trending toward the buttress, to reach the base of Cutthroat Wall.

road.name: SR-20 to Cutthroat Trailhead via Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400)
road.status: Paved highway to a maintained USFS trailhead with kiosk and paved/graded parking; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; parking is limited and fills quickly
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April; the trailhead itself is inaccessible whenever the highway is closed
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). From Winthrop, continue west on SR-20 about 28 miles (roughly 1.5 mi west of Lone Fir Campground), then turn right (north) onto Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400) and follow it about 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

routes.approach: Note: this area's stored coordinates place it at Washington Pass on SR-20, but the real-world Gunsight Range is a remote granite sub-range near Dome Peak in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, roughly 20+ miles south/southwest of Washington Pass — not reachable from the SR-20 pass corridor, likely a data/geocoding error worth flagging for correction. The genuine standard approach is from the Downey Creek Trailhead (off Suiattle River Road) via the southern end of the Ptarmigan Traverse — a rugged, largely off-trail alpine route over Itswoot Ridge/Cub Lake and Bachelor Creek involving glacier travel and significant bushwhacking. Climbers typically budget a full day (or two) of approach before reaching the base of South Peak's granite faces; this is an experienced-mountaineers-only objective, not a roadside crag.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.status: Gravel/paved forest road from Darrington; washed out roughly 4.5 miles in, with a Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF closure order in effect — vehicle access ends at the washout, foot/bike travel possible beyond it
road.seasonalGate: Effectively gated at the mile-4.5 washout; call the Darrington Ranger District to confirm current drivable distance before a trip
road.driveNote: From Darrington, WA: ~8 miles north on SR-530, then east on FR-26 (Suiattle River Road) roughly 20 miles toward Downey Creek Trailhead. Vehicles currently cannot reach the trailhead due to the washout, adding several miles of road-walking/biking to the approach.

routes.approach: Note: this area's stored coordinates place it at Washington Pass on SR-20, matching South Peak's identical (likely erroneous) coordinates — the real Gunsight Range sits ~20+ miles south/southwest near Dome Peak. Middle Peak shares the same approach as South Peak: from Downey Creek Trailhead, follow the southern Ptarmigan Traverse cross-country route (Itswoot Ridge/Cub Lake, Bachelor Creek) to reach the range's ridge. Middle Gunsight's east face is a known technical objective once on the ridge; expect a multi-day glaciated approach with route-finding and bushwhacking, not a drive-up or short-trail access.

routes.approach: From the pullout, a short (~5-minute) woods trail leads to a large boulder/scree field at the base of the slope. From there, ascend and angle left for about 15 minutes to reach a mossy ramp system that marks the start of the wall. The route up the ramp climbs the mossy slab's far-left side, diagonaling toward the top right where a fixed rappel anchor (inspect before trusting) allows descent; a full 60m rope skips the mossy slab entirely, landing back at the scree field. Best conditions are summer when the slab is dry. Osprey Wall is a small, lightly-documented crag, so treat these approach specifics as generally reliable but not pinpoint-precise until verified on site.

road.name: I-90 (Snoqualmie Pass corridor, Exits 52-54)
road.status: Paved interstate, open year-round; subject to WSDOT traction-tire/chain requirements and occasional temporary closures during severe winter storms
road.driveNote: Take I-90 east or west to the Snoqualmie Pass area (Exits 52-54) and follow the local frontage road toward the Snoqualmie Pass community. Park at a small pullout on the west side of the road (space for only 2-3 cars) — the exact pullout is easy to miss and best confirmed against a current Mountain Project description before the trip.

routes.approach: From the NF-77 pullout, a well-used trail runs along the base of the formation. The West Face and North Side are different aspects of the same rock reached via short spurs off this main trail, generally under 15 minutes' walk from the parking pullout.

road.name: NF-25 / NF-28 / NF-77 (Gifford Pinchot National Forest roads)
road.status: Open seasonally; NF-77 is a rough, narrow forest road for the final stretch — high-clearance recommended, drive slowly
road.driveNote: From Randle, WA (Hwy 12), head south on Rt. 131 then follow NF-25 south about 19-23 miles. Past mile marker 21, turn left at the sign for Mosquito Meadows onto NF-28 and follow it about 2.7 miles, then turn onto NF-77 and continue roughly 2 miles to a large pullout where the trail to the formation is visible. No formal gate, but the primitive roads can stay snow-blocked into late spring.
