routes.approach: From the Barlow Pass gate, walk the closed road (old railroad grade) approximately 4 miles to the Monte Cristo townsite; a backcountry campground sits at that mark. Mossy Loaf's coordinates place it essentially at/immediately adjacent to the townsite itself, so once at the flat of Monte Cristo the formation is only a short distance further off the main townsite path. The exact final spur to the specific wall isn't well documented in public sources, so on-site scouting or a guidebook is recommended for the last stretch.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Barlow Pass, then the closed Monte Cristo Road (foot/bike only)
road.status: Mountain Loop Highway is paved to Barlow Pass; beyond the Barlow Pass gate, the old Monte Cristo road has been closed to motor vehicles for years due to washouts and is foot/bike travel only
road.seasonalGate: The Barlow Pass vehicle gate is a long-standing closure, not a seasonal one. The Mountain Loop Highway itself can have separate winter snow closures on the unpaved middle section further east; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before the trip.
road.driveNote: From Granite Falls, follow the Mountain Loop Highway east about 31 paved miles to Barlow Pass. Park at the Barlow Pass trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass required).

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive to Mount Rainier National Park via the Nisqually Entrance, through Longmire and Paradise, then east on Stevens Canyon Road about 2.7 miles to the Snow Lake Trailhead. From the trailhead, follow the maintained Snow Lake Trail roughly 1 mile up through subalpine forest and meadows to Snow Lake at the base of the Tatoosh Range. From the lake, leave the maintained trail and climb steep boot-path/scree slopes (often snow-covered into midsummer) up into the upper basin and gully system toward the saddle just west of Unicorn Peak's summit block. The final push wraps around the west side of the summit tower and finishes with a short, exposed Class 3-4 (occasionally low 5th class) scramble/rock step of roughly 50 feet to the true summit. Total round trip is commonly cited around 5-6 miles with roughly 2,400-2,700 feet of elevation gain; a rope, harness, and basic rock gear are recommended by many trip reports.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road
road.status: paved, two-lane National Park road; passable to standard passenger vehicles when open
road.seasonalGate: closed in winter/spring; typically opens around Memorial Day and closes at the first major snowfall of the season (usually mid-October); also prone to temporary closures from flooding/rockfall
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 S to SR-512 E, then SR-7 S through Elbe to SR-706 E through Ashford to the Nisqually Entrance of Mount Rainier National Park. Continue past Longmire to the Paradise junction, then turn right (east) onto Stevens Canyon Road and drive about 2.7 miles, passing Reflection Lakes, to the signed Snow Lake Trailhead pullout. Drive from Seattle takes roughly 2.5-3 hours. An NPS entrance pass is required.

routes.approach: The standard approach shares the trailhead used for Mount Ellinor, high above Lake Cushman in the southeast Olympics. From the road-end trailhead a climber's path climbs steeply through forest and meadow before splitting toward Washington's east-side routes, most commonly the East Ridge, which involves Class 3-4 rock, a short rappel into a notch, and a final Class 4 pitch to the 6,255-ft summit. Many parties combine Washington with neighboring Mount Ellinor in a single ridge traverse. There are also less-traveled north/west side routes reached via Forest Road 25 (Hamma Hamma) and FR 2401 to the Elk Lakes area.

road.name: Forest Road 2419 (via North Lake Cushman Rd / FR 24)
road.status: gravel Forest Service road, generally passable in a standard vehicle in summer/fall
road.seasonalGate: the upper stretch can be snowed in or gated a few miles below the trailhead in winter/early spring
road.driveNote: From US-101 in Hoodsport, turn west onto SR-119 (North Lake Cushman Road) and drive about 9.4 miles to a T intersection; turn right onto Forest Road 24 for roughly 1.5 miles, then left onto Forest Road 2419 and follow it about 6.6 miles up the Big Creek drainage to its end at the Mount Ellinor/Mount Washington trailhead (about 3,000 ft).

routes.approach: Mount Olympus, the highest peak in the Olympics and its most famous objective, is reached via the Hoh River Trail starting at the Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center. The trail is a long, largely gentle rainforest walk for its first many miles, passing Happy Four Camp, the Olympus Guard Station, and Elk Lake before climbing more steeply over the final push to Glacier Meadows high camp at roughly 17 miles one-way and about 4,200 ft elevation. From Glacier Meadows, climbers rope up and continue onto the Blue Glacier, ascending the Snow Dome to reach the summit pyramid, which requires glacier travel and some technical rock/snow near the top. The final two miles of trail below Glacier Meadows have historically been affected by slides and washouts, so current conditions should be checked with the visitor center.

road.name: Upper Hoh Road
road.status: paved, two-lane, generally well-maintained and open year-round
road.driveNote: From Port Angeles, follow US-101 west/south around the Olympic Peninsula toward Forks; about 12.5 miles south of Forks turn east onto Upper Hoh Road and continue roughly 19 miles to its end at the Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center. The Hoh River corridor has experienced periodic flood/washout damage, so check current NPS road status before driving.

routes.approach: The standard approach to The Brothers' south peak (Mt. Edward) starts at the Lena Lake Trailhead off Hamma Hamma River Road. Hikers follow the Lena Lake Trail about 3 miles to Lower Lena Lake, then pick up a climber's path around the lake and up through the Valley of the Silent Men toward a high camp near the East Fork Lena Creek basin, roughly another 3 miles. From camp, the route ascends the South Couloir, exiting right around 5,000 ft onto an open rocky slope known as Lunch Rock, then follows an exposed ridge (steep and sometimes corniced in early season) to the south summit. Round trip is on the order of 15 miles with roughly 6,200 ft of elevation gain and loss, and the route requires mountaineering gear and experience with steep snow/rock.

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25)
road.status: gravel Forest Service road, generally maintained but can have rough or washed-out sections
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport, drive north on US-101 about 14 miles to around milepost 318, then turn west (left) onto Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25) and continue roughly 8-9.5 miles to the Lena Lake Trailhead (about 685-700 ft).

routes.approach: The standard approach to The Brothers' south peak (Mt. Edward) starts at the Lena Lake Trailhead off Hamma Hamma River Road. Hikers follow the Lena Lake Trail about 3 miles to Lower Lena Lake, then pick up a climber's path around the lake and up through the Valley of the Silent Men toward a high camp near the East Fork Lena Creek basin, roughly another 3 miles. From camp, the route ascends the South Couloir, exiting right around 5,000 ft onto an open rocky slope known as Lunch Rock, then follows an exposed ridge (steep and sometimes corniced in early season) to the south summit. Round trip is on the order of 15 miles with roughly 6,200 ft of elevation gain and loss, and the route requires mountaineering gear and experience with steep snow/rock.

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25)
road.status: gravel Forest Service road, generally maintained but can have rough or washed-out sections
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport, drive north on US-101 about 14 miles to around milepost 318, then turn west (left) onto Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25) and continue roughly 8-9.5 miles to the Lena Lake Trailhead (about 685-700 ft).

routes.approach: Whatcom Peak is one of the most remote summits in the Picket Range and demands a genuine multi-day wilderness expedition, not a day approach. From Seattle, drive I-5 and WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) roughly 2.5-3 hours to the Ross Dam Trailhead near milepost 134, which is only reliably open outside the winter closure season. From there, hike down to Ross Lake and either walk the lakeshore trail (adding several extra miles) or arrange a paid water taxi through Ross Lake Resort (reservations required, roughly late June through October) across the lake to the Big Beaver Trail landing. From Big Beaver Landing, follow the Big Beaver and Whatcom Pass trails through old-growth forest and up into the alpine, a stretch commonly covered in two backpacking days each way, before continuing off-trail via Perfect Pass to reach Whatcom Peak's upper slopes and ridge. Total round-trip mileage is commonly cited in the 30-40+ mile range with substantial elevation gain, and parties typically budget several days to a week. For this specific route: from the Whatcom Pass camp, head directly south and climb the north ridge — a roughly 1-hour 3rd-class scramble straight up from the pass.

road.name: WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Ross Dam Trailhead
road.status: paved and maintained year-round from the west (Seattle/I-5 via Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, Marblemount, Newhalem) to the Ross Dam Trailhead at milepost 134
road.seasonalGate: WA-20 closes for winter (typically early December to mid-spring, timing varies with snowpack and repair work) at the Ross Dam Trailhead gate, cutting off the route east over Rainy and Washington Passes — the Ross Dam Trailhead itself stays reachable from the west side even when the highway is gated beyond it
road.driveNote: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Sedro-Woolley/Burlington, then WA-20 east roughly 130-140 miles (about 2.5-3 hours) through Concrete, Marblemount, and Newhalem to the Ross Dam Trailhead parking area at milepost 134; check WSDOT road conditions before relying on this route outside summer/fall.

routes.approach: Whatcom Peak is one of the most remote summits in the Picket Range and demands a genuine multi-day wilderness expedition, not a day approach. From Seattle, drive I-5 and WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) roughly 2.5-3 hours to the Ross Dam Trailhead near milepost 134, which is only reliably open outside the winter closure season. From there, hike down to Ross Lake and either walk the lakeshore trail (adding several extra miles) or arrange a paid water taxi through Ross Lake Resort (reservations required, roughly late June through October) across the lake to the Big Beaver Trail landing. From Big Beaver Landing, follow the Big Beaver and Whatcom Pass trails through old-growth forest and up into the alpine, a stretch commonly covered in two backpacking days each way, before continuing off-trail via Perfect Pass to reach Whatcom Peak's upper slopes and ridge. Total round-trip mileage is commonly cited in the 30-40+ mile range with substantial elevation gain, and parties typically budget several days to a week. For this specific route: from the same pass/camp, traverse in a low orbit around the east side of the peak (or via the alternate Easy Ridge-to-Perfect Pass approach) to reach the south side, then ascend a long snowfield to an exposed class 3 scramble along the south ridge crest — same base camp as North Ridge, but a materially different direction and distance.

road.name: WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) to Ross Dam Trailhead
road.status: paved and maintained year-round from the west (Seattle/I-5 via Sedro-Woolley, Concrete, Marblemount, Newhalem) to the Ross Dam Trailhead at milepost 134
road.seasonalGate: WA-20 closes for winter (typically early December to mid-spring, timing varies with snowpack and repair work) at the Ross Dam Trailhead gate, cutting off the route east over Rainy and Washington Passes — the Ross Dam Trailhead itself stays reachable from the west side even when the highway is gated beyond it
road.driveNote: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Sedro-Woolley/Burlington, then WA-20 east roughly 130-140 miles (about 2.5-3 hours) through Concrete, Marblemount, and Newhalem to the Ross Dam Trailhead parking area at milepost 134; check WSDOT road conditions before relying on this route outside summer/fall.

routes.approach: Spire Gully right (Alpenkuhl), part of the Hairpin Crags, is reached from the same large pullout at the hairpin turns on SR-20 near Washington Pass used for Kangaroo Temple. Looking up toward South Early Winter Spire from the pullout, Spire Gully is the obvious gully just below and left; Alpenkuhl is its sunnier, south-facing right side (the left side is known as Tiger Wall). The approach is one of the shortest at Washington Pass — a direct walk/scramble of roughly 10-15 minutes straight up from the highway, snow-covered in early season and a worn boot path later in summer.

road.name: SR-20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: Paved 2-lane highway; open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Washington Pass closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary by year); gated near Ross Dam (MP ~134) and Early Winters/Silver Star (MP ~171-178) depending on conditions
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass, ~170-180 mi / 3-3.5 hrs. From Winthrop/Mazama: ~20-25 min west on SR-20.

routes.approach: This is a distinct approach from the one used for Cutthroat Peak, which starts from an unmarked highway pullout about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass with no maintained trail. For Cutthroat Wall, start on the maintained Cutthroat Creek Trail #483 from the Cutthroat Trailhead kiosk. After a short distance on the main trail, leave it via a cairned path that cuts into the forest, then follow cairns and a climbers' path (roughly 1.5-2 miles, 45 min-2 hrs total) through brush and talus, trending toward the buttress, to reach the base of Cutthroat Wall.

road.name: SR-20 to Cutthroat Trailhead via Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400)
road.status: Paved highway to a maintained USFS trailhead with kiosk and paved/graded parking; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; parking is limited and fills quickly
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April; the trailhead itself is inaccessible whenever the highway is closed
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). From Winthrop, continue west on SR-20 about 28 miles (roughly 1.5 mi west of Lone Fir Campground), then turn right (north) onto Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400) and follow it about 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

routes.approach: This is a distinct approach from the one used for Cutthroat Peak, which starts from an unmarked highway pullout about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass with no maintained trail. road.name: SR-20 to Cutthroat Trailhead via Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400)
road.status: Paved highway to a maintained USFS trailhead with kiosk and paved/graded parking; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; parking is limited and fills quickly
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April; the trailhead itself is inaccessible whenever the highway is closed
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). From Winthrop, continue west on SR-20 about 28 miles (roughly 1.5 mi west of Lone Fir Campground), then turn right (north) onto Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400) and follow it about 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

routes.approach: Primus Peak is reached by driving Cascade River Road from Marblemount (paved to about mile 10, then gravel) to the Eldorado trailhead parking area around mile 20, the same access point used for Eldorado Peak. From there, climbers ford the Cascade River and follow the Eldorado Creek drainage, but instead of turning up to the Eldorado Glacier basin, continue past Eldorado toward Rouch Creek and onto the Inspiration/McAllister Icecap and North Klawatti/Klawatti/Inspiration Glacier system, with several miles of glacier travel to reach Primus vicinity (e.g., Lucky Pass) or approach via the Inspiration-Isolation Traverse from the Thunder Creek/North Cascades Highway side. This is a serious, remote multi-day glacier mountaineering objective — most parties camp on a glacier or moraine (commonly cited around 6,000-6,500 ft) and spend 2-4+ days on crevassed terrain; the summit climbing itself is easy, but the approach length, river crossing, and glacier navigation are the crux. Because of this, Primus is very rarely climbed alone and is most often tagged as part of the longer Inspiration-Isolation Traverse linking Eldorado, Klawatti, Dorado Needle, Austera, and Isolation.

road.name: Cascade River Road
road.status: Paved for the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then rough gravel; opens seasonally (typically late June/early July) to the Eldorado parking area (mile 20) and on to Cascade Pass Trailhead (mile 23.1), weather and washouts permitting.
road.seasonalGate: Gated/closed in winter and during storm damage; road is not maintained for large vehicles beyond the Eldorado parking area (mile 20).
road.driveNote: From Marblemount on WA-20, drive Cascade River Road roughly 19-20 miles to the Eldorado trailhead parking area (~2,150 ft) — the same trailhead used for Eldorado Peak.

routes.approach: From the NF-77 pullout, a well-used trail runs along the base of the formation. The West Face and North Side are different aspects of the same rock reached via short spurs off this main trail, generally under 15 minutes' walk from the parking pullout.

road.name: NF-25 / NF-28 / NF-77 (Gifford Pinchot National Forest roads)
road.status: Open seasonally; NF-77 is a rough, narrow forest road for the final stretch — high-clearance recommended, drive slowly
road.driveNote: From Randle, WA (Hwy 12), head south on Rt. 131 then follow NF-25 south about 19-23 miles. Past mile marker 21, turn left at the sign for Mosquito Meadows onto NF-28 and follow it about 2.7 miles, then turn onto NF-77 and continue roughly 2 miles to a large pullout where the trail to the formation is visible. No formal gate, but the primitive roads can stay snow-blocked into late spring.

routes.approach: From the NF-77 pullout, a well-used trail runs along the base of the formation. The West Face and North Side are different aspects of the same rock reached via short spurs off this main trail, generally under 15 minutes' walk from the parking pullout.

road.name: NF-25 / NF-28 / NF-77 (Gifford Pinchot National Forest roads)

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington, then WA-20 east through Marblemount, and turn onto Cascade River Road, following its paved-then-gravel surface about 21.7 miles to a small pullout at 3,200 ft that marks the unmaintained Boston Basin Trailhead (a different, lower stop than the Eldorado trailhead a mile or so up-road, and just before the road's end at Cascade Pass). From there a steep, unmaintained climbers' path follows the old Diamond Mine roadbed briefly before climbing hard, crossing several streams, and breaking above treeline (~5,300 ft) to reach the popular Boston Basin high camp around 6,000 ft — roughly 3-4 miles one-way with about 3,000-3,300 ft of elevation gain. From camp, West Ridge climbers continue cross-country over the glacier to a couloir that gains the ridge crest. Because Boston Basin is a high-demand cross-country camping zone in North Cascades National Park, overnight parties need a wilderness permit; a portion of peak-season (mid-May to mid-October) slots can be reserved in advance via Recreation.gov's early-access lottery, with the remainder issued as limited first-come, first-served walk-up permits. For this specific route: from Boston Basin high camp, ascend the Quien Sabe Glacier toward the Boston-Sahale col, then traverse to the base of Sharkfin Tower's southeast side.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Paved for the first stretch out of Marblemount, then becomes gravel/washboard with potholes for the final miles; passable to most cars in good conditions but a high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially near road's end.
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed each winter (often near Eldorado Creek, roughly MP 20); NPS/Forest Service typically reopen it in late spring (around late May-June) once snow and storm damage are cleared, so early-season access can be blocked or delayed by washouts.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 north to Burlington (exit ~230), then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles to a small pullout at ~3,200 ft marking the unsigned Boston Basin Trailhead, just short of the road's end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~23 miles).

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Paved for the first stretch out of Marblemount, then becomes gravel/washboard with potholes for the final miles; passable to most cars in good conditions but a high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially near road's end.
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed each winter (often near Eldorado Creek, roughly MP 20); NPS/Forest Service typically reopen it in late spring (around late May-June) once snow and storm damage are cleared, so early-season access can be blocked or delayed by washouts.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 north to Burlington (exit ~230), then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles to a small pullout at ~3,200 ft marking the unsigned Boston Basin Trailhead, just short of the road's end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~23 miles).

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington, then WA-20 east through Marblemount, and turn onto Cascade River Road, following its paved-then-gravel surface about 21.7 miles to a small pullout at 3,200 ft that marks the unmaintained Boston Basin Trailhead (a different, lower stop than the Eldorado trailhead a mile or so up-road, and just before the road's end at Cascade Pass). From there a steep, unmaintained climbers' path follows the old Diamond Mine roadbed briefly before climbing hard, crossing several streams, and breaking above treeline (~5,300 ft) to reach the popular Boston Basin high camp around 6,000 ft — roughly 3-4 miles one-way with about 3,000-3,300 ft of elevation gain. From camp, West Ridge climbers continue cross-country over the glacier to a couloir that gains the ridge crest. Because Boston Basin is a high-demand cross-country camping zone in North Cascades National Park, overnight parties need a wilderness permit; a portion of peak-season (mid-May to mid-October) slots can be reserved in advance via Recreation.gov's early-access lottery, with the remainder issued as limited first-come, first-served walk-up permits. For this specific route: from Boston Basin high camp, traverse the upper basin northwest beneath Forbidden Peak's south spur to the lower South Ridge and South Col bivy sites, then up the western couloir to the start of the climb.

routes.approach: IMPORTANT: Johannesberg Mountain does NOT use the Boston Basin trailhead or high camp, unlike its Boston Basin neighbors. The approach is from the Cascade Pass trailhead / 'hairpin' turn on Cascade River Road: descend and ford the Cascade River directly, climb up and over the terminal moraine, and trend toward a snow finger (~3,500ft) leading onto the buttress/ridge. This is a distinct, more direct, and notably harder bushwhack/scramble approach than the other Boston Basin peaks.

road.name: Cascade River Road to Cascade Pass Trailhead / hairpin turn
road.status: Paved for the first ~10 miles from Marblemount, then gravel/washboard; passable to most cars in good conditions but high-clearance recommended near road's end
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated/closed by snow in winter and opens seasonally in late spring/early summer once plowed
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road to the hairpin turn near the road's end (Cascade Pass Trailhead area) — NOT the separate Boston Basin trailhead pullout used by the neighboring Boston Basin peaks.

road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive Cascade River Road to the hairpin turn near the road's end (Cascade Pass Trailhead area) — NOT the separate Boston Basin trailhead pullout used by the neighboring Boston Basin peaks.

From there a steep, unmaintained climbers' path follows the old Diamond Mine roadbed briefly before climbing hard, crossing several streams, and breaking above treeline (~5,300 ft) to reach the popular Boston Basin high camp around 6,000 ft — roughly 3-4 miles one-way with about 3,000-3,300 ft of elevation gain. routes.approach: From Seattle, drive to Mount Rainier National Park via the Nisqually Entrance, through Longmire and Paradise, then east on Stevens Canyon Road about 2.7 miles to the Snow Lake Trailhead. From the trailhead, follow the maintained Snow Lake Trail roughly 1 mile up through subalpine forest and meadows to Snow Lake at the base of the Tatoosh Range. From the lake, leave the maintained trail and climb steep boot-path/scree slopes (often snow-covered into midsummer) up into the upper basin and gully system toward the saddle just west of Unicorn Peak's summit block. The final push wraps around the west side of the summit tower and finishes with a short, exposed Class 3-4 (occasionally low 5th class) scramble/rock step of roughly 50 feet to the true summit. Total round trip is commonly cited around 5-6 miles with roughly 2,400-2,700 feet of elevation gain; a rope, harness, and basic rock gear are recommended by many trip reports. For this specific route: from the Snow Lake/Pinnacle Saddle trail, head directly up the south-facing SW ridge and a class 3 gully to the summit block — the shortest, most direct line, not passing by the Castle-Pinnacle saddle.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road
road.status: paved, two-lane National Park road; passable to standard passenger vehicles when open
road.seasonalGate: closed in winter/spring; typically opens around Memorial Day and closes at the first major snowfall of the season (usually mid-October); also prone to temporary closures from flooding/rockfall
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 S to SR-512 E, then SR-7 S through Elbe to SR-706 E through Ashford to the Nisqually Entrance of Mount Rainier National Park. Continue past Longmire to the Paradise junction, then turn right (east) onto Stevens Canyon Road and drive about 2.7 miles, passing Reflection Lakes, to the signed Snow Lake Trailhead pullout. Drive from Seattle takes roughly 2.5-3 hours. An NPS entrance pass is required.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive to Mount Rainier National Park via the Nisqually Entrance, through Longmire and Paradise, then east on Stevens Canyon Road about 2.7 miles to the Snow Lake Trailhead. From the trailhead, follow the maintained Snow Lake Trail roughly 1 mile up through subalpine forest and meadows to Snow Lake at the base of the Tatoosh Range. From the lake, leave the maintained trail and climb steep boot-path/scree slopes (often snow-covered into midsummer) up into the upper basin and gully system toward the saddle just west of Unicorn Peak's summit block. The final push wraps around the west side of the summit tower and finishes with a short, exposed Class 3-4 (occasionally low 5th class) scramble/rock step of roughly 50 feet to the true summit. Total round trip is commonly cited around 5-6 miles with roughly 2,400-2,700 feet of elevation gain; a rope, harness, and basic rock gear are recommended by many trip reports. For this specific route: continue past the standard SW scramble start to the saddle between The Castle and Pinnacle Peak, then rope up for class 4 to 5.6 pitches up the East Ridge — a longer, more technical final approach than the direct SW scramble.

road.status: paved, two-lane National Park road; passable to standard passenger vehicles when open
road.seasonalGate: closed in winter/spring; typically opens around Memorial Day and closes at the first major snowfall of the season (usually mid-October); also prone to temporary closures from flooding/rockfall

routes.approach: From Bellingham, drive SR-542 (Mount Baker Highway) east about 46 miles to its terminus near Artist Point, parking at the paved Lake Ann Trailhead lot roughly a mile before the road's end; the last 2.7 miles past Heather Meadows are gated shut every winter (commonly mid/late October to June), so the trailhead is unreachable by car outside the summer season. From the trailhead, a maintained trail descends through subalpine forest and meadows to Lake Ann (about 4 miles round-trip to the lake basin), then a climbers' path continues up to the Lower Curtis Glacier and the base of the Fisher Chimneys; parties scramble/climb the chimneys to Winnies Slide and cross the White Salmon, Upper Curtis, and Sulphide glaciers toward the summit pyramid, with the full round trip commonly reported around 15-16 miles and roughly 6,750-7,000 ft of cumulative elevation gain. Most of the route lies within North Cascades National Park, so any overnight stay requires a backcountry/climbing permit.

road.name: Mount Baker Highway (SR-542)
road.status: paved to the trailhead
road.seasonalGate: final 2.7 miles from Heather Meadows to Artist Point close for winter (typically mid-to-late October) and reopen in June; the Lake Ann Trailhead lot sits within this seasonally-gated stretch, about 1 mile before road's end
road.driveNote: From Bellingham, take SR-542 (Mount Baker Highway) east roughly 46 miles to its end near Artist Point; from Seattle, take I-5 north to Bellingham (exit 255) then SR-542 east, about 2.5-3 hours total. The paved Lake Ann Trailhead parking lot is on the east side of SR-542 about a mile before Artist Point, near the Mount Baker Ski Area.

routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Campground trailhead near Newhalem, climbers follow an unmaintained, boot-worn path roughly 4 to 4.5 miles up the forested Goodell Creek valley before turning uphill on a brutally steep, brushy side path that gains around 1,500 feet in well under half a mile. Above treeline the route makes a rising traverse across heather and scree to a notch near 6,200 feet overlooking Terror Basin, which serves as the standard base camp. From camp, reaching West McMillan Spire itself requires crossing a permanent snowfield/glacier remnant (crampons and an ice axe are mandatory) before finishing on exposed Class 2-3 scrambling to the summit. This is a genuine multi-day expedition-style approach; a North Cascades National Park backcountry permit is required, and there is significant routefinding and rockfall/glacier hazard throughout.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Goodell Creek Road/Campground, Newhalem
road.status: paved highway open year-round to Newhalem; Goodell Creek Road is a short gravel spur open in season
road.driveNote: WA-20 is open year-round as far as Newhalem (the highway's seasonal closure gate is much farther east near Washington Pass/Mazama); from Newhalem a short gravel road leads to Goodell Creek Campground, where the maintained road ends and all further travel is on foot.

routes.approach: From the Goodell Creek Campground trailhead near Newhalem, climbers follow an unmaintained, boot-worn path roughly 4 to 4.5 miles up the forested Goodell Creek valley before turning uphill on a brutally steep, brushy side path that gains around 1,500 feet in well under half a mile. Above treeline the route makes a rising traverse across heather and scree to a notch near 6,200 feet overlooking Terror Basin, which serves as the standard base camp. From camp, reaching West McMillan Spire itself requires crossing a permanent snowfield/glacier remnant (crampons and an ice axe are mandatory) before finishing on exposed Class 2-3 scrambling to the summit. This is a genuine multi-day expedition-style approach; a North Cascades National Park backcountry permit is required, and there is significant routefinding and rockfall/glacier hazard throughout.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Goodell Creek Road/Campground, Newhalem
road.status: paved highway open year-round to Newhalem; Goodell Creek Road is a short gravel spur open in season
road.driveNote: WA-20 is open year-round as far as Newhalem (the highway's seasonal closure gate is much farther east near Washington Pass/Mazama); from Newhalem a short gravel road leads to Goodell Creek Campground, where the maintained road ends and all further travel is on foot.

routes.approach: Inspiration Peak shares the same approach as West McMillan Spire: from Goodell Creek Campground, an unmaintained path climbs the Goodell Creek valley for roughly 4 to 4.5 miles before a steep, brushy climbers' track gains about 1,500 feet in under half a mile to reach the alpine notch above Terror Basin, the standard base camp for the Southern Pickets. From camp, reaching Inspiration Peak involves traversing heather and snow slopes and crossing a permanent snowfield near the col between it and West McMillan Spire before tackling a technical rock route (low 5th class up to 5.9 depending on the line). This is a serious multi-day expedition approach requiring a North Cascades National Park backcountry permit, off-trail routefinding through brush and talus, and glacier/snow travel gear. For this specific route: reached directly from the Terror Glacier, climbing straight up the prominent 'gash' feature on the face overhanging the glacier — the shortest, most direct line from the ice.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Goodell Creek Road/Campground, Newhalem
road.status: paved highway open year-round to Newhalem; Goodell Creek Road is a short gravel spur open in season
road.driveNote: WA-20 is open year-round as far as Newhalem (the highway's seasonal closure gate is much farther east near Washington Pass/Mazama); from Newhalem a short gravel road leads to Goodell Creek Campground, where the maintained road ends and all further travel is on foot.

routes.approach: Inspiration Peak shares the same approach as West McMillan Spire: from Goodell Creek Campground, an unmaintained path climbs the Goodell Creek valley for roughly 4 to 4.5 miles before a steep, brushy climbers' track gains about 1,500 feet in under half a mile to reach the alpine notch above Terror Basin, the standard base camp for the Southern Pickets. From camp, reaching Inspiration Peak involves traversing heather and snow slopes and crossing a permanent snowfield near the col between it and West McMillan Spire before tackling a technical rock route (low 5th class up to 5.9 depending on the line). This is a serious multi-day expedition approach requiring a North Cascades National Park backcountry permit, off-trail routefinding through brush and talus, and glacier/snow travel gear. For this specific route: continue picking a way further across Terror Glacier to reach the base of the ridge on the peak's east side — a modest but real extra distance/direction beyond where the South Face route stops.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Goodell Creek Road/Campground, Newhalem
road.status: paved highway open year-round to Newhalem; Goodell Creek Road is a short gravel spur open in season
road.driveNote: WA-20 is open year-round as far as Newhalem (the highway's seasonal closure gate is much farther east near Washington Pass/Mazama); from Newhalem a short gravel road leads to Goodell Creek Campground, where the maintained road ends and all further travel is on foot.

routes.approach: Drive SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Ross Dam Trailhead, then descend roughly 15-20 minutes to Ross Lake, where most parties arrange a paid water taxi from Ross Lake Resort (or paddle/walk) to the Big Beaver Trail landing rather than hiking the long lakeshore trail. From Big Beaver Landing it's about 13-14 miles of trail up the Big Beaver valley to Beaver Pass, a full day's hike with a loaded pack. From there, routes diverge onto Wiley Ridge or over Whatcom Pass/Easy Ridge/Perfect Pass to gain the upper basins beneath the Challenger Glacier, with substantial off-trail travel through brush, talus, and heather slopes. The final push crosses the crevassed Challenger Glacier to a short summit rock pitch; this is a genuine multi-day (5+ days) glacier mountaineering expedition into one of the most remote, weather-exposed corners of the North Cascades, with essentially no bailout options once committed.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134)
road.status: paved and maintained in season; subject to occasional closures from rockfall/storm damage
road.seasonalGate: closed in winter, typically early December through April or May, at gates near Ross Dam/Newhalem/Diablo due to snow and avalanche danger
road.driveNote: From the Seattle area it's roughly a 3-hour drive east on SR-20 to the Ross Dam Trailhead; check WSDOT/NPS road-status pages before going since the highway closes seasonally and has had multi-year rockslide closures near Diablo Lake.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134)
road.status: paved and maintained in season; subject to occasional closures from rockfall/storm damage
road.seasonalGate: closed in winter, typically early December through April or May, at gates near Ross Dam/Newhalem/Diablo due to snow and avalanche danger
road.driveNote: From the Seattle area it's roughly a 3-hour drive east on SR-20 to the Ross Dam Trailhead; check WSDOT/NPS road-status pages before going since the highway closes seasonally and has had multi-year rockslide closures near Diablo Lake.

routes.approach: From the Rainy Pass Trailhead on SR-20, take the Lake Ann/Heather Pass Trail, staying right at the Lake Ann junction and continuing up switchbacks to Heather Pass (about 2.5 miles in, near 6,200 ft), where the maintained trail bends toward Maple Pass and a climbers' path splits off toward Wing Lake. Descend and traverse roughly 200 ft above Lewis Lake across a couple of talus/rockslide crossings, skirt the lake's south side, then climb through larch meadows and moraine to Wing Lake, tucked directly beneath Black Peak's south face. From Wing Lake, ascend the peak's south slopes/couloir (the standard, non-technical route, Class 2-3 with some loose rock and possible snow early season) to the summit ridge and final scramble to the 8,970 ft summit. The round trip is a long day, commonly cited at 10-12 hours for fit parties. For this specific route: from Wing Lake camp, hike east around the lake, up the moraine onto a permanent snowfield to an obvious col in the south ridge; a broad gully on the west side of the col leads to the summit blocks.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: paved and open in season; the section through Rainy Pass/Washington Pass closes every winter for avalanche danger
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed roughly early December through April/May between the Ross Dam (MP 134) and Silver Star (MP 171) gates, sometimes retracting further to the Newhalem and Early Winters gates in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: Drive SR-20 to the Rainy Pass Trailhead (Lake Ann/Heather Pass/Maple Pass trailhead) near milepost 157, at roughly 4,855 ft elevation, with a large paved parking area and NW Forest Pass/park pass required.

routes.approach: From the Rainy Pass Trailhead on SR-20, take the Lake Ann/Heather Pass Trail, staying right at the Lake Ann junction and continuing up switchbacks to Heather Pass (about 2.5 miles in, near 6,200 ft), where the maintained trail bends toward Maple Pass and a climbers' path splits off toward Wing Lake. Descend and traverse roughly 200 ft above Lewis Lake across a couple of talus/rockslide crossings, skirt the lake's south side, then climb through larch meadows and moraine to Wing Lake, tucked directly beneath Black Peak's south face. From Wing Lake, ascend the peak's south slopes/couloir (the standard, non-technical route, Class 2-3 with some loose rock and possible snow early season) to the summit ridge and final scramble to the 8,970 ft summit. The round trip is a long day, commonly cited at 10-12 hours for fit parties. For this specific route: from the same Wing Lake camp, head instead to a heather bench below the snowfield on the east face, ascend the snowfield then loose class 3 terrain to an ~8,000 ft notch in the NE Ridge — a genuinely different direction and higher target notch than South Ridge.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20)
road.status: paved and open in season; the section through Rainy Pass/Washington Pass closes every winter for avalanche danger
road.seasonalGate: Typically closed roughly early December through April/May between the Ross Dam (MP 134) and Silver Star (MP 171) gates, sometimes retracting further to the Newhalem and Early Winters gates in heavy-snow years
road.driveNote: Drive SR-20 to the Rainy Pass Trailhead (Lake Ann/Heather Pass/Maple Pass trailhead) near milepost 157, at roughly 4,855 ft elevation, with a large paved parking area and NW Forest Pass/park pass required.

routes.approach: Drive I-90 to Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass and park at the PCT North trailhead. From there follow the Pacific Crest Trail north for roughly 5.5-6 miles, passing Kendall Katwalk and Ridge Lake, to a steep unmarked side-trail climbing toward Bumblebee Pass. Cross the pass and descend into the basin south of Mount Thomson, then ascend heather and talus to a notch on the peak's ESE ridge for the final short class 3-4 scramble to the summit. Total approach is roughly 8 miles one-way with about 3,600 ft of gain, commonly done as a long day or an overnight. For this specific route: from the basin below Bumblebee Pass, contour northwest to a notch at the base of the West Ridge (~5,960 ft), where the first low-5th-class pitch begins.

road.name: I-90 to the PCT North Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass (Exit 52)
road.status: paved and maintained year-round to the trailhead parking lot
road.driveNote: From North Bend, take I-90 east to Exit 52, cross under the freeway, and follow signs a short distance to the large paved Pacific Crest Trail North trailhead lot; a Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee is required.

routes.approach: Drive I-90 to Exit 52 at Snoqualmie Pass and park at the PCT North trailhead. From there follow the Pacific Crest Trail north for roughly 5.5-6 miles, passing Kendall Katwalk and Ridge Lake, to a steep unmarked side-trail climbing toward Bumblebee Pass. Cross the pass and descend into the basin south of Mount Thomson, then ascend heather and talus to a notch on the peak's ESE ridge for the final short class 3-4 scramble to the summit. Total approach is roughly 8 miles one-way with about 3,600 ft of gain, commonly done as a long day or an overnight. For this specific route: from the same basin, angle the opposite direction (east) up across a rock field toward the east gully/ridge, following a climbers' trail through heather to a notch (~5,600 ft) on Thomson's ESE Ridge.

road.name: I-90 to the PCT North Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass (Exit 52)
road.status: paved and maintained year-round to the trailhead parking lot
road.driveNote: From North Bend, take I-90 east to Exit 52, cross under the freeway, and follow signs a short distance to the large paved Pacific Crest Trail North trailhead lot; a Northwest Forest Pass or day-use fee is required.

routes.approach: From the Dutch Miller Gap Trailhead, follow Dutch Miller Gap Trail 1030 up the Middle Fork Snoqualmie valley about 14-15 miles to Dutch Miller Gap, passing Hardscrabble Horse Camp and the Williams Lake trail junction along the way. Little Big Chief sits immediately southwest of the gap, reached by leaving the trail near Williams Lake for an off-trail scramble up the peak's east slope/northeast ridge, with a short but exposed class 4 notch as the crux below the summit. An alternate, longer approach comes from the east via the Necklace Valley Trail off Foss River Road (Skykomish/US 2 side), crossing La Bohn Gap before dropping to Dutch Miller Gap. For this specific route: leave the main Middle Fork Snoqualmie trail at a small steel bridge well before Dutch Miller Gap and cut cross-country south into Summit Chief Valley; the Northwest Face is visible on the peak's east side across that valley, roughly 3 hours from the trailhead — a distinctly different final leg than the Northeast Face's direct ridge approach.

road.name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road (Forest Service Road 56) to the Dingford Creek/Dutch Miller Gap Trailhead
road.status: mostly gravel and rough beyond the pavement, high-clearance vehicle recommended; the road has a history of washouts and repair closures along the river
road.seasonalGate: sections of FS-56 have been gated or closed after storm damage in the past — check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts before driving
road.driveNote: From North Bend take I-90 to Exit 34, go north on 468th Ave SE about 0.6 miles, then turn right onto Middle Fork Rd/FS-56 and continue roughly 17 miles (pavement giving way to rough gravel) to the trailhead.

routes.approach: From the Dutch Miller Gap Trailhead, follow Dutch Miller Gap Trail 1030 up the Middle Fork Snoqualmie valley about 14-15 miles to Dutch Miller Gap, passing Hardscrabble Horse Camp and the Williams Lake trail junction along the way. Little Big Chief sits immediately southwest of the gap, reached by leaving the trail near Williams Lake for an off-trail scramble up the peak's east slope/northeast ridge, with a short but exposed class 4 notch as the crux below the summit. An alternate, longer approach comes from the east via the Necklace Valley Trail off Foss River Road (Skykomish/US 2 side), crossing La Bohn Gap before dropping to Dutch Miller Gap. For this specific route: continue the standard Middle Fork Snoqualmie trail all the way up to Dutch Miller Gap, then climb directly above the gap onto the peak's northeast ridge/face — no cross-country detour into Summit Chief Valley needed.

road.name: Middle Fork Snoqualmie River Road (Forest Service Road 56) to the Dingford Creek/Dutch Miller Gap Trailhead
road.status: mostly gravel and rough beyond the pavement, high-clearance vehicle recommended; the road has a history of washouts and repair closures along the river
road.seasonalGate: sections of FS-56 have been gated or closed after storm damage in the past — check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest alerts before driving
road.driveNote: From North Bend take I-90 to Exit 34, go north on 468th Ave SE about 0.6 miles, then turn right onto Middle Fork Rd/FS-56 and continue roughly 17 miles (pavement giving way to rough gravel) to the trailhead.

routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 54 (Hyak), follow Hyak Drive onto Forest Service Road NF-9070, continuing past where the pavement ends to the junction at Ollallie Meadow where the Pacific Crest Trail crosses the road. Take the left fork of NF-9070 and follow it until the road terminates in a large turnaround just below Silver Basin. From that turnaround, it's roughly a 20-30 minute approach on foot up into Silver Basin to reach the base of The Citadel's walls, including Sofa King Buttress on the NW side of the basin's east wall. The road is not plowed and is generally only clear of snow from around July through November.

road.name: Forest Service Road NF-9070 (from Hyak Drive, I-90 Exit 54)
road.status: unpaved; rough with exposed rocks and water bars past the pavement's end — high-clearance vehicle recommended
road.seasonalGate: No permanent gate, but the road is snowbound and effectively impassable outside roughly July-November; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving
road.driveNote: Leave I-90 at Exit 54 (Hyak), take Hyak Drive to FS Road NF-9070, and follow it past the pavement's end to Ollallie Meadow where the PCT crosses the road; bear left at that fork and continue to the large turnaround just below Silver Basin.

routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 54 (Hyak), follow Hyak Drive onto Forest Service Road NF-9070, continuing past where the pavement ends to the junction at Ollallie Meadow where the Pacific Crest Trail crosses the road. routes.approach: From Naches, take US-12 west roughly 23 miles to just past the Rimrock Lake Dam, then turn south onto Tieton Reservoir Road (FR-1200). For the popular rock routes on the eastern cliffs, park in one of the pullouts along Tieton Road near a spring marked by a split-rail fence and follow the steep climbers' trail uphill to the base of the routes. To reach the summit via the non-technical Southwest Ridge, continue on FR-1200 to the South Fork/campground turnoff, park near the restroom area, and hike cross-country, following criss-crossing boot-paths while staying on the ridge crest until it becomes an established path to the summit block. The area melts out quickly and is climbable from May through November.

road.name: Tieton Reservoir Road (Forest Road 1200)
road.status: Paved from US-12 to the reservoir area, becoming gravel near campgrounds/pullouts; generally well-maintained and passable to passenger cars in season
road.seasonalGate: Closed by snow roughly November through May; typically reopens late May and stays open through October
road.driveNote: From Naches, WA drive west on US-12 (White Pass Highway) about 23 miles to just east of the Rimrock Lake Dam, then turn south onto Tieton Reservoir Road (FR-1200); a Bureau of Reclamation project has caused intermittent through-travel closures on the segment between FR-1203 and FR-1000, so check current USFS conditions.

routes.approach: Drive to Tiffany Springs Campground (elev. ~6,760 ft) via Forest Road 39, accessed either from Conconully (south, via FR-37) or from Winthrop (north, via East Chewuch Road and FR-37) — both are long gravel-road drives of an hour or more. From the campground, hike the Tiffany Lake Trail #373 east past Tiffany Lake to Honeymoon Pass (7,150 ft, roughly 45 minutes of easy trail), then leave the trail and traverse south across open grassy slopes to the small cirque at the base of the North Face (about 7,360 ft). The North Face itself rises roughly 650 feet above the cirque, split by couloirs and a rock tower; the whole approach is considered short and easy relative to other Pasayten-area objectives.

road.name: Forest Road 39 (Tiffany Road), reached via FR-37 from Conconully or via East Chewuch Road/FR-37 from Winthrop
road.status: Unpaved, rough gravel/dirt Forest Service road; passable to most vehicles in dry conditions but not graded regularly, high-clearance recommended, and trailers are prohibited on the final stretch into Tiffany Springs Campground
road.seasonalGate: No formal gate, but the road is snowed in through winter/spring and typically doesn't open until snow clears in early-to-mid summer
road.driveNote: From Conconully it's roughly a 30+ mile, ~1.5-2 hour drive south via FR-37 then north on FR-39 to Tiffany Springs Campground; from Winthrop it's a similarly long backroads drive north via East Chewuch Road and FR-37/FR-39.

routes.approach: Note: this area's stored coordinates place it at Washington Pass on SR-20, but the real-world Gunsight Range is a remote granite sub-range near Dome Peak in the Glacier Peak Wilderness, roughly 20+ miles south/southwest of Washington Pass — not reachable from the SR-20 pass corridor, likely a data/geocoding error worth flagging for correction. The genuine standard approach is from the Downey Creek Trailhead (off Suiattle River Road) via the southern end of the Ptarmigan Traverse — a rugged, largely off-trail alpine route over Itswoot Ridge/Cub Lake and Bachelor Creek involving glacier travel and significant bushwhacking. Climbers typically budget a full day (or two) of approach before reaching the base of South Peak's granite faces; this is an experienced-mountaineers-only objective, not a roadside crag.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26)
road.status: Gravel/paved forest road from Darrington; washed out roughly 4.5 miles in, with a Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF closure order in effect — vehicle access ends at the washout, foot/bike travel possible beyond it
road.seasonalGate: Effectively gated at the mile-4.5 washout; call the Darrington Ranger District to confirm current drivable distance before a trip
road.driveNote: From Darrington, WA: ~8 miles north on SR-530, then east on FR-26 (Suiattle River Road) roughly 20 miles toward Downey Creek Trailhead. Vehicles currently cannot reach the trailhead due to the washout, adding several miles of road-walking/biking to the approach.

routes.approach: From the pullout, find a faint boot path descending loose talus/scree to the east into the trees, passing a trail register, then crossing Early Winters Creek on a log (~4,080 ft). The trail climbs the timbered rib north of Burgundy Creek, steeply gaining elevation to a basin/camp bench around 6,500 ft (roughly 2 hours from the car), then continues up to Burgundy Col (~7,720 ft, ~3 hours). Burgundy Spire is reached from the basin/col area with a short additional scramble; good camping and a late-season spring are near 6,400-6,500 ft.

road.name: SR-20 (North Cascades Highway)
road.status: paved highway, seasonally closed
road.seasonalGate: Closed roughly mid/late November to mid/late April for avalanche danger; WSDOT's early-season gates are Ross Dam (MP134) and the Silver Star gate (MP171), later retreating to Newhalem (MP120) and Early Winters (MP178).
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass/Washington Pass, ~170-180 mi/3-3.5 hrs. Park at an unmarked pullout on the east side of the highway about 4 miles east of Washington Pass (near milepost 166, ~4,250-4,310 ft).

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then east on SR-20 through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass; from Winthrop/Mazama it's a quick 20-30 minute drive west on SR-20 instead. Park at the Blue Lake Trailhead (about 5,200 ft, Northwest Forest Pass required), on the south side of the highway roughly 1.5 miles west of the Washington Pass overlook. Follow the Blue Lake Trail #314 about 1.5 miles to a small meadow, then branch off left onto the well-worn climbers' path, which climbs steeply through slabs, talus, and meadow (with short 3rd-class scrambling) into the basin below the Liberty Bell Group — roughly 3 miles and 2,000 ft of gain, 1.5-2.5 hours total. The classic Beckey Route starts from the notch (about 7,300 ft) between Liberty Bell and Concord Tower, reached via the gully splitting off the climbers' path.

road.name: WA-20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: seasonal; open roughly late April/May through November
road.seasonalGate: Closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary year to year with snowpack); winter closure gates sit near Ross Dam TH (west side) and Mazama (east side).
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount, Newhalem, and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass — roughly 170-180 miles, about 3-3.5 hours nonstop. From the east side, it's a short 20-30 minute drive west from Winthrop/Mazama on SR-20.

routes.approach: The Temple (Kangaroo Temple, on Kangaroo Ridge) is reached from the large pullout at the hairpin turns on SR-20 just east of Washington Pass — the same parking area used for the neighboring Hairpin Crags/Spire Gully climbs. From the pullout, a climber's trail heads south across a boulder field, through forest and more talus (faint, intermittently cairned) up to Kangaroo Pass, about 1 hour. From the pass, an intermittent trail traverses to a gully between Kangaroo Temple and Little Finger; ascending or descending that gully reaches the base of the North Face, Northwest Face, or Southwest Face routes, roughly another hour. Total trailhead-to-summit time runs about 7-8 hours round trip.

road.name: SR-20 / North Cascades Highway
road.status: Paved 2-lane highway; open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Washington Pass closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary by year); gated near Ross Dam (MP ~134) and Early Winters/Silver Star (MP ~171-178) depending on conditions
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass, ~170-180 mi / 3-3.5 hrs. From Winthrop/Mazama: ~20-25 min west on SR-20.

routes.approach: This is a distinct approach from the one used for Cutthroat Peak, which starts from an unmarked highway pullout about 1.5 miles west of Washington Pass with no maintained trail. For Cutthroat Wall, start on the maintained Cutthroat Creek Trail #483 from the Cutthroat Trailhead kiosk. After a short distance on the main trail, leave it via a cairned path that cuts into the forest, then follow cairns and a climbers' path (roughly 1.5-2 miles, 45 min-2 hrs total) through brush and talus, trending toward the buttress, to reach the base of Cutthroat Wall.

road.name: SR-20 to Cutthroat Trailhead via Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400)
road.status: Paved highway to a maintained USFS trailhead with kiosk and paved/graded parking; day-use fee or Northwest Forest Pass required; parking is limited and fills quickly
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes for winter avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April; the trailhead itself is inaccessible whenever the highway is closed
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount and Rainy Pass (~170-180 mi, 3-3.5 hrs). From Winthrop, continue west on SR-20 about 28 miles (roughly 1.5 mi west of Lone Fir Campground), then turn right (north) onto Cutthroat Creek Road (FS Road 400) and follow it about 1 mile to the Cutthroat Trailhead.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then east on SR-20 through Marblemount and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass; from Winthrop/Mazama it's a quick 20-30 minute drive west on SR-20 instead. Park at the Blue Lake Trailhead (about 5,200 ft, Northwest Forest Pass required), on the south side of the highway roughly 1.5 miles west of the Washington Pass overlook. Follow the Blue Lake Trail #314 about 1.5 miles to a small meadow, then branch off left onto the well-worn climbers' path, which climbs steeply through slabs, talus, and meadow (with short 3rd-class scrambling) into the basin below the Liberty Bell Group — roughly 3 miles and 2,000 ft of gain, 1.5-2.5 hours total. road.seasonalGate: Closes every winter for avalanche danger, typically mid/late November through mid/late April (exact dates vary year to year with snowpack); winter closure gates sit near Ross Dam TH (west side) and Mazama (east side).
road.driveNote: From Seattle: I-5 N to Burlington/Sedro-Woolley, then SR-20 E through Marblemount, Newhalem, and over Rainy Pass to Washington Pass — roughly 170-180 miles, about 3-3.5 hours nonstop. From the east side, it's a short 20-30 minute drive west from Winthrop/Mazama on SR-20.

routes.approach: Inspiration Peak shares the same approach as West McMillan Spire: from Goodell Creek Campground, an unmaintained path climbs the Goodell Creek valley for roughly 4 to 4.5 miles before a steep, brushy climbers' track gains about 1,500 feet in under half a mile to reach the alpine notch above Terror Basin, the standard base camp for the Southern Pickets. From camp, reaching Inspiration Peak involves traversing heather and snow slopes and crossing a permanent snowfield near the col between it and West McMillan Spire before tackling a technical rock route (low 5th class up to 5.9 depending on the line). This is a serious multi-day expedition approach requiring a North Cascades National Park backcountry permit, off-trail routefinding through brush and talus, and glacier/snow travel gear.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (WA-20) to Goodell Creek Road/Campground, Newhalem
road.status: paved highway open year-round to Newhalem; Goodell Creek Road is a short gravel spur open in season
road.driveNote: WA-20 is open year-round as far as Newhalem (the highway's seasonal closure gate is much farther east near Washington Pass/Mazama); from Newhalem a short gravel road leads to Goodell Creek Campground, where the maintained road ends and all further travel is on foot.

routes.approach: Drive SR-20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Ross Dam Trailhead, then descend roughly 15-20 minutes to Ross Lake, where most parties arrange a paid water taxi from Ross Lake Resort (or paddle/walk) to the Big Beaver Trail landing rather than hiking the long lakeshore trail. From Big Beaver Landing it's about 13-14 miles of trail up the Big Beaver valley to Beaver Pass, a full day's hike with a loaded pack. From there, routes diverge onto Wiley Ridge or over Whatcom Pass/Easy Ridge/Perfect Pass to gain the upper basins beneath the Challenger Glacier, with substantial off-trail travel through brush, talus, and heather slopes. The final push crosses the crevassed Challenger Glacier to a short summit rock pitch; this is a genuine multi-day (5+ days) glacier mountaineering expedition into one of the most remote, weather-exposed corners of the North Cascades, with essentially no bailout options once committed.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to the Ross Dam Trailhead (MP 134)
road.status: paved and maintained in season; subject to occasional closures from rockfall/storm damage
road.seasonalGate: closed in winter, typically early December through April or May, at gates near Ross Dam/Newhalem/Diablo due to snow and avalanche danger
road.driveNote: From the Seattle area it's roughly a 3-hour drive east on SR-20 to the Ross Dam Trailhead; check WSDOT/NPS road-status pages before going since the highway closes seasonally and has had multi-year rockslide closures near Diablo Lake.

routes.approach: From Seattle, drive I-5 north to Burlington, then WA-20 east through Marblemount, and turn onto Cascade River Road, following its paved-then-gravel surface about 21.7 miles to a small pullout at 3,200 ft that marks the unmaintained Boston Basin Trailhead (a different, lower stop than the Eldorado trailhead a mile or so up-road, and just before the road's end at Cascade Pass). From there a steep, unmaintained climbers' path follows the old Diamond Mine roadbed briefly before climbing hard, crossing several streams, and breaking above treeline (~5,300 ft) to reach the popular Boston Basin high camp around 6,000 ft — roughly 3-4 miles one-way with about 3,000-3,300 ft of elevation gain. From camp, West Ridge climbers continue cross-country over the glacier to a couloir that gains the ridge crest. Because Boston Basin is a high-demand cross-country camping zone in North Cascades National Park, overnight parties need a wilderness permit; a portion of peak-season (mid-May to mid-October) slots can be reserved in advance via Recreation.gov's early-access lottery, with the remainder issued as limited first-come, first-served walk-up permits.

road.name: Cascade River Road (Forest Road 15)
road.status: Paved for the first stretch out of Marblemount, then becomes gravel/washboard with potholes for the final miles; passable to most cars in good conditions but a high-clearance vehicle is recommended, especially near road's end.
road.seasonalGate: Gated and closed each winter (often near Eldorado Creek, roughly MP 20); NPS/Forest Service typically reopen it in late spring (around late May-June) once snow and storm damage are cleared, so early-season access can be blocked or delayed by washouts.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-5 north to Burlington (exit ~230), then WA-20 (North Cascades Highway) east to Marblemount, then follow Cascade River Road roughly 21.7 miles to a small pullout at ~3,200 ft marking the unsigned Boston Basin Trailhead, just short of the road's end at the Cascade Pass Trailhead (~23 miles).

routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 52, follow Alpental Road up to the ski area and park along Erste Strasse near the base chalets, taking care to respect posted no-parking signs on the private-property sections of the road. For the south-face/rock routes, the crag rises directly above the parking area with essentially no approach hike required. For routes on other aspects of the mountain, use the Commonwealth Basin/PCT North Trailhead a short distance up the highway instead, following the Pacific Crest Trail into Commonwealth Basin before cutting up toward the peak. Because of the short, direct access, Guye Peak is climbed both summer and winter, though rock routes are primarily a snow-free-season (summer-fall) objective.

road.name: Alpental Road (Road 9041) via I-90 Exit 52, Snoqualmie Pass
road.status: Paved and open year-round, maintained for Alpental ski area access
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-90 east about 30-45 minutes to Exit 52 (Snoqualmie Pass/Alpental), then follow Alpental Road/Road 9041 toward the ski area, parking in pullouts along Erste Strasse near the base chalets, directly below Guye Peak's south face.

routes.approach: From I-90 Exit 54 (Hyak), follow Hyak Drive onto Forest Service Road NF-9070, continuing past where the pavement ends to the junction at Ollallie Meadow where the Pacific Crest Trail crosses the road. Take the left fork of NF-9070 and follow it until the road terminates in a large turnaround just below Silver Basin. From that turnaround, it's roughly a 20-30 minute approach on foot up into Silver Basin to reach the base of The Citadel's walls, including Sofa King Buttress on the NW side of the basin's east wall. The road is not plowed and is generally only clear of snow from around July through November.

road.name: Forest Service Road NF-9070 (from Hyak Drive, I-90 Exit 54)
road.status: unpaved; rough with exposed rocks and water bars past the pavement's end — high-clearance vehicle recommended
road.seasonalGate: No permanent gate, but the road is snowbound and effectively impassable outside roughly July-November; check current Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving
road.driveNote: Leave I-90 at Exit 54 (Hyak), take Hyak Drive to FS Road NF-9070, and follow it past the pavement's end to Ollallie Meadow where the PCT crosses the road; bear left at that fork and continue to the large turnaround just below Silver Basin.

routes.approach: From Leavenworth, drive west on US-2 to the edge of town, turn onto Icicle Creek Road, go about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto Forest Service Road 7601 and follow its rough, high-clearance-recommended dirt surface roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead. From there, the on-foot approach follows Trail #1599 for about 2.5 miles to a junction, then climbs via switchbacks roughly another 1.5 miles to Colchuck Lake (about 4 miles and ~2,200 ft of gain one-way, at 5,570 ft elevation) — already a strenuous day-hike in its own right. From the lake, climbers continue to the base of Aasgard Pass and grind up its infamous, largely unmaintained, boulder-and-scree slope, gaining roughly 2,000-2,300 ft in under a mile to the 7,800 ft pass, before dropping into the Core Enchantment Zone where Prusik Peak's iconic spire (near Gnome Tarn) comes into view. This is one of the most demanding standard approaches in the Cascades and is commonly done as a very long day or split over two days. Overnight camping anywhere in the Enchantment Permit Area (including the Core Zone) requires a highly competitive lottery permit via Recreation.gov, with applications typically open in a roughly two-week window in mid-to-late February for the May 15-October 31 permit season. Day hiking/climbing without camping does not require the lottery and instead only needs a free, self-issued day-use permit obtained at the trailhead.

road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Service Road 7601 (Stuart Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FS Road 7601 is unpaved, rough, and often washboarded/potholed — high-clearance vehicles recommended
road.seasonalGate: FS 7601 is gated and closed in winter, typically from around November through May depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow US-2 to the west edge of town and turn onto Icicle Creek Road (signed for the Leavenworth National Fish Hatchery); drive about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto FS Road 7601 and follow the rough dirt road roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

From there, the on-foot approach follows Trail #1599 for about 2.5 miles to a junction, then climbs via switchbacks roughly another 1.5 miles to Colchuck Lake (about 4 miles and ~2,200 ft of gain one-way, at 5,570 ft elevation) — already a strenuous day-hike in its own right. road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Service Road 7601 (Stuart Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FS Road 7601 is unpaved, rough, and often washboarded/potholed — high-clearance vehicles recommended
road.seasonalGate: FS 7601 is gated and closed in winter, typically from around November through May depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow US-2 to the west edge of town and turn onto Icicle Creek Road (signed for the Leavenworth National Fish Hatchery); drive about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto FS Road 7601 and follow the rough dirt road roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: This route is reached from the OPPOSITE side of the mountain via the Stuart Lake Trailhead, not the Teanaway/Longs Pass side used for Sherpa Peak's West Ridge. From the Stuart Lake Trailhead, hike the Stuart Lake Trail about 1.5 miles past the first switchback, then leave the trail and cut left (south) onto a boot path, crossing Stuart Lake's outlet creek and following Mountaineer Creek upstream with the creek on your left. Above treeline the path becomes a cairned line through talus on the slope west of the creek, leading into the basin below Sherpa Peak's north side. The North Ridge rises in a blocky jumble starting from the last trees around 7,000 ft. Plan roughly 4-5 hours from the trailhead to the base of the route.

road.name: Icicle Road (FR-7600) / Eightmile Road (FR-7601)
road.status: seasonal — open summer/fall, gated in winter-spring
road.seasonalGate: Eightmile Road gate typically closed early November through late spring to protect the roadbed from snow damage; open for the summer/fall climbing season
road.driveNote: From US-2 just west of Leavenworth, turn onto Icicle Road (FR-7600) and follow it roughly 8.5 miles to Bridge Creek Campground, then continue on Eightmile Road (FR-7601) about 4 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,600 ft). Watch for temporary closures from rockfall/erosion sections around MP 4-11.

routes.approach: The standard approach for most Mt Stuart routes, including the classic North Ridge, starts at the Longs Pass/Esmeralda Basin/Ingalls Lake trailhead at the end of North Fork Teanaway Road, reached from Seattle via I-90 to Cle Elum, then Teanaway Road/North Fork Teanaway Road (paved for about the first 13 miles, gravel FR 9737 for the last stretch) to the road's end near 4,200-4,300 ft, requiring a Northwest Forest Pass. From the trailhead, climbers hike a short old mining road to the Longs Pass/Ingalls Way junction, then climb over Longs Pass or Ingalls Pass, pass Lake Ingalls, and traverse east below the Stuart Range crest to Stuart Pass before ascending to Goat Pass (about 7,650 ft) and dropping onto the Stuart Glacier to reach the base of the North Ridge — a long, serious approach on the order of 7-9 miles one-way with roughly 3,500-4,500 ft of gain to a typical high camp near Goat Pass or the upper basin below the West Ridge. The Ingalls Creek Trailhead, reached via US-97 over Blewett Pass and Ingalls Creek Road, offers an alternate southern approach up the Ingalls Creek valley to Stuart Pass, but at about 16 miles one-way it is considerably longer and is used less often, mainly by parties heading directly to the south side/Cascadian Couloir or using it as a descent option. For this specific route: same Esmeralda Trailhead/Longs Pass/Ingalls Creek approach used for Mt Stuart's standard routes — over Longs Pass, down to Ingalls Creek, upstream to the Beverly-Turnpike Trail junction, then up-slope to the Stuart-Sherpa col.

road.name: Teanaway Road / North Fork Teanaway Road to Forest Road 9737 (Esmeralda/Longs Pass–Ingalls Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 13-14 miles from Hwy 970 (Teanaway Road, then North Fork Teanaway Road); becomes a maintained gravel forest road (FR 9737) for the final several miles to the trailhead. High-clearance not required in dry conditions, but washboard and potholes are common on the gravel section.
road.seasonalGate: No formal locked gate, but the road and trail sit at 4,200+ ft and are typically snowed in through spring; the trailhead/basin usually isn't fully clear of snow on the trail until late June, sometimes later in a heavy snow year.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-90 east to Exit 85 (Cle Elum), then Hwy 970 east about 7-8.5 miles to a left onto Teanaway Road. Follow it roughly 13 miles (pavement ends here) onto North Fork Teanaway Road, then continue on gravel FR 9737 to its end at the Esmeralda Basin/Longs Pass/Ingalls Lake trailhead. A Northwest Forest Pass is required to park.

routes.approach: This route is reached via the Icicle Creek side, not the Teanaway/Beverly Creek side used for Argonaut's South Face. From the Stuart Lake Trailhead, follow the Stuart Lake Trail about 3 miles to where Mountaineer Creek enters, then leave the trail, cross the creek, and pick up a boot path climbing along the west side of Mountaineer Creek. Continue up the drainage to where the valley forks at about 4,800 ft (roughly 3-4 hours from the trailhead), then work toward the basin north of Argonaut's summit, where bivy sites sit at 6,390 ft and 6,600 ft. From that basin, ascend a snow finger angling climber's-right (southwest) to a notch at about 7,700 ft, where the Northwest Arete's rock climbing begins — the opposite side of the peak from the Teanaway/Beverly Creek approach.

road.name: Icicle Road (FR-7600) / Eightmile Road (FR-7601)
road.status: seasonal — open summer/fall, gated in winter-spring
road.seasonalGate: Eightmile Road gate typically closed early November through late spring; open for the summer/fall climbing season
road.driveNote: From US-2 just west of Leavenworth, turn onto Icicle Road (FR-7600) and follow it roughly 8.5 miles to Bridge Creek Campground, then continue on Eightmile Road (FR-7601) about 4 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead (3,600 ft). Watch for temporary closures from rockfall/erosion sections around MP 4-11.

routes.approach: The standard approach for most Mt Stuart routes, including the classic North Ridge, starts at the Longs Pass/Esmeralda Basin/Ingalls Lake trailhead at the end of North Fork Teanaway Road, reached from Seattle via I-90 to Cle Elum, then Teanaway Road/North Fork Teanaway Road (paved for about the first 13 miles, gravel FR 9737 for the last stretch) to the road's end near 4,200-4,300 ft, requiring a Northwest Forest Pass. From the trailhead, climbers hike a short old mining road to the Longs Pass/Ingalls Way junction, then climb over Longs Pass or Ingalls Pass, pass Lake Ingalls, and traverse east below the Stuart Range crest to Stuart Pass before ascending to Goat Pass (about 7,650 ft) and dropping onto the Stuart Glacier to reach the base of the North Ridge — a long, serious approach on the order of 7-9 miles one-way with roughly 3,500-4,500 ft of gain to a typical high camp near Goat Pass or the upper basin below the West Ridge. The Ingalls Creek Trailhead, reached via US-97 over Blewett Pass and Ingalls Creek Road, offers an alternate southern approach up the Ingalls Creek valley to Stuart Pass, but at about 16 miles one-way it is considerably longer and is used less often, mainly by parties heading directly to the south side/Cascadian Couloir or using it as a descent option. For this specific route: standard Teanaway approach via the Beverly Creek/Bean Creek Trailhead off North Fork Teanaway River Road — hike the Beverly and Fourth Creek trails about 6 miles to a ford of Ingalls Creek, then scramble up to the base of the South Face.

road.name: Teanaway Road / North Fork Teanaway Road to Forest Road 9737 (Esmeralda/Longs Pass–Ingalls Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Paved for roughly the first 13-14 miles from Hwy 970 (Teanaway Road, then North Fork Teanaway Road); becomes a maintained gravel forest road (FR 9737) for the final several miles to the trailhead. High-clearance not required in dry conditions, but washboard and potholes are common on the gravel section.
road.seasonalGate: No formal locked gate, but the road and trail sit at 4,200+ ft and are typically snowed in through spring; the trailhead/basin usually isn't fully clear of snow on the trail until late June, sometimes later in a heavy snow year.
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-90 east to Exit 85 (Cle Elum), then Hwy 970 east about 7-8.5 miles to a left onto Teanaway Road. Follow it roughly 13 miles (pavement ends here) onto North Fork Teanaway Road, then continue on gravel FR 9737 to its end at the Esmeralda Basin/Longs Pass/Ingalls Lake trailhead. A Northwest Forest Pass is required to park.
