road.name: Icicle Creek Road to Forest Service Road 7601 (Stuart Lake Trailhead)
road.status: Icicle Creek Road is paved; FS Road 7601 is unpaved, rough, and often washboarded/potholed — high-clearance vehicles recommended
road.seasonalGate: FS 7601 is gated and closed in winter, typically from around November through May depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Leavenworth, follow US-2 to the west edge of town and turn onto Icicle Creek Road (signed for the Leavenworth National Fish Hatchery); drive about 8.4 miles, then turn left onto FS Road 7601 and follow the rough dirt road roughly 3.7 miles to the Stuart Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: The standard approach to The Brothers' south peak (Mt. Edward) starts at the Lena Lake Trailhead off Hamma Hamma River Road. Hikers follow the Lena Lake Trail about 3 miles to Lower Lena Lake, then pick up a climber's path around the lake and up through the Valley of the Silent Men toward a high camp near the East Fork Lena Creek basin, roughly another 3 miles. From camp, the route ascends the South Couloir, exiting right around 5,000 ft onto an open rocky slope known as Lunch Rock, then follows an exposed ridge (steep and sometimes corniced in early season) to the south summit. Round trip is on the order of 15 miles with roughly 6,200 ft of elevation gain and loss, and the route requires mountaineering gear and experience with steep snow/rock.

road.name: Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25)
road.status: gravel Forest Service road, generally maintained but can have rough or washed-out sections
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport, drive north on US-101 about 14 miles to around milepost 318, then turn west (left) onto Hamma Hamma River Road (Forest Road 25) and continue roughly 8-9.5 miles to the Lena Lake Trailhead (about 685-700 ft).

routes.approach: From the Upper Dungeness/Royal Basin Trailhead (elev. ~2,500 ft), hike the Upper Dungeness River Trail roughly 7 miles with about 2,500 ft of gain to Royal Lake, then continue about a mile farther into Upper Royal Basin (~5,600 ft), a meadow-and-tarn basin ringed by Deception, Mystery, Fricaba, and Hal Foss Peak. From the upper basin, leave the trail and ascend scree/talus into Deception Basin, then climb the left (west) of two gullies just right of a large rock buttress. Follow that gully system up to Mount Deception's east ridge about 150 yards southeast of the summit and follow the ridge to the top; the scramble from Deception Basin takes roughly 3 hours. A Northwest Forest Pass is needed at the trailhead and a wilderness permit is required for overnight stays.

road.name: Palo Alto Road to Forest Road 2880/2870 (Upper Dungeness Trailhead)
road.status: open, good dirt road
road.driveNote: From US-101 near Sequim Bay, take Louella Rd then Palo Alto Rd west about 6 miles until it becomes FR 2880; follow FR 2880 across the Dungeness River, turn left onto FR 2870, and continue roughly 6.5-9 miles to the large trailhead parking area just past the river bridge (~2,500 ft).

routes.approach: From Staircase Ranger Station, follow the old road along the North Fork Skokomish River about 2.7 miles to a bridge crossing, then continue on the Flapjack Lakes Trail roughly 3.5-4.1 miles to Flapjack Lakes, gaining about 3,300 ft total. From Flapjack Lakes, climb the trail toward Gladys Divide and into the Sawtooth Range, where a snow gully below Needle Pass (often icy, requiring crampons and an ice axe most of the year) leads up toward Cruiser Pass. Mount Cruiser itself has no scramble route to the summit — the easiest line is low 5th class with sustained exposure. Overnight permits are required for backcountry camps in Olympic National Park.

road.name: Lake Cushman Road (WA-119) to Forest Road 24 (Staircase Ranger Station)
road.status: open as of 2026 (reopened after prior fire-damage closure)
road.driveNote: From Hoodsport on US-101, take Lake Cushman Rd/WA-119 north about 9 miles to FR 24, then continue west along the north shore of Lake Cushman about 6.5 miles to the Staircase Ranger Station trailhead. This is the actual access point for Mount Cruiser — not the separate Elk Lake Trail near Hamma Hamma, which does not reach the peak.

routes.approach: From the parking pullout on spur 270, an unmarked climbers' path ascends directly up the boulder field on the hillside toward Tyler Peak Crags; there is no formal trailhead sign. The Full Montey sits on the upper bench of the Tyler Peak crag system, reached by climbing up past the base of the adjacent Mossness wall/sector to gain the upper bench. Because the approach trail is unofficial and unsigned, most parties rely on Mountain Project's area description or GPS beta rather than a marked trail; expect a steep, brushy bushwhack-and-boulder-hop of a few hundred vertical feet from the road.

road.name: Forest Roads 2880/2870/2860 to Spur 270 (Tyler Peak Crags)
road.status: open, rough/unmaintained final spur
road.driveNote: From US-101 take Palo Alto Rd or Louella Rd to FR 2880, cross the Dungeness River bridge where the road continues as FR 2870, then as it becomes FR 2860. Turn right onto spur road '270' and drive about 1.2 miles of rough road strewn with boulders — high-clearance advisable — to a boulder field visible on the hillside, informal parking.

routes.approach: From the Barlow Pass trailhead, hike or mountain-bike the old, gated Monte Cristo Road roughly 4-4.5 miles to the Monte Cristo ghost town. From the townsite, follow the Glacier Basin Trail for under a mile before leaving trail to ascend gullies and rock bands (or a steep snow gully, condition-dependent) up toward the basin below the Wilmans Spires, needle-like formations dividing Seventy-six Basin and Glacier Basin. All summit routes are fifth-class rock, so this is a full climbing day layered on top of an already long approach.

road.name: Mountain Loop Highway to Barlow Pass, then Monte Cristo Road (gravel, gated)
road.status: Mountain Loop Highway is paved/gravel and open seasonally to Barlow Pass; Monte Cristo Road beyond the gate has been permanently closed to motor vehicles since 1980 flood damage, foot/bike only
road.seasonalGate: Mountain Loop Highway is gated for winter (typically late November through late May); best access is July-October
road.driveNote: Drive Mountain Loop Highway from Verlot to the Barlow Pass trailhead (Northwest Forest Pass required), then continue on foot or mountain bike along the closed Monte Cristo Road.

routes.approach: Dome Peak is normally reached either from the north via the Downey Creek Trailhead or, more commonly for a full traverse, from the south end of the classic Ptarmigan Traverse starting at Cascade Pass in North Cascades National Park. From Downey Creek, the trail runs about 6.6 miles to Bachelor Creek, where it meets the off-trail Ptarmigan Traverse route. Either direction involves multiple days of glacier travel, off-trail routefinding, and significant elevation gain/loss — a committing, multi-day glaciated objective requiring full crevasse-rescue and off-trail navigation skills.

road.name: Suiattle River Road (Forest Road 26) to Downey Creek Trailhead
road.status: Gravel forest road with a long history of flood damage and multi-year closures; prone to washout closures that can last years
road.driveNote: When open, drive SR 530 north from Darrington about 7.5 miles, turn onto FR 26 (Suiattle River Road), and follow pavement then gravel roughly 20.6 miles to the Downey Creek trailhead just past the Downey Creek bridge; a Northwest Forest Pass is required. Check current Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest road-status alerts before planning a trip.

routes.approach: From Holden Village, hike west along the Railroad Creek valley trail about a mile to the Holden Lake Trail junction, then continue roughly 4 miles up to Holden Lake or on to Holden Pass for a high camp. From there, routes to Bonanza Peak (9,511 ft, Washington's highest peak outside a national park and highest non-volcanic summit in the state) typically continue via the Mary Green Glacier or the Holden Pass/Company Glacier approach. Between the ferry-and-bus logistics and the long valley/glacier approach beyond it, this is one of the most logistically complex and remote climbing objectives in Washington, generally requiring 3+ days car-to-car with real glacier travel and exposed 5th-class climbing near the summit.

road.name: No direct road access — reached via Lake Chelan ferry to Lucerne, then bus/hike to Holden Village
road.status: No public road reaches Holden Village or the Railroad Creek valley; access is by boat only, operated by the Lake Chelan Boat Company
road.seasonalGate: Ferry runs on a published seasonal schedule (reduced frequency in winter); confirm current sailing days/times before a trip
road.driveNote: Drive to Chelan or Field's Point Landing on Lake Chelan, then take the passenger ferry (no vehicles) uplake to the Lucerne dock. From Lucerne, ride the Holden Village shuttle bus (or hike) roughly 11-13 miles up to Holden Village; there is no drive-in option.

routes.approach: From the FR 1570 parking area (~1,250 ft), hike the blocked, overgrown roadbed about 3 miles to where a steep way trail begins (~2,300 ft) at the edge of an old clearcut. The way trail climbs steeply to a knoll around 5,116 ft, staying east of the knoll before dropping to a saddle and continuing SSW along Kindly Found Ridge to a knoll near 5,791 ft. From there climbers continue toward Found Lake and the NE Shoulder to reach Snowking's summit. This is an unmaintained, brushy, way-trail approach with route-finding required above treeline.

road.name: Cascade River Road (FR 15) to Forest Road 1570 (Kindy Creek/Found Creek spur)
road.status: Cascade River Road is paved/good gravel and passable to nearly all vehicles as far as the Cascade River crossing; FR 1570 beyond that is rough, narrow, and prone to washouts near the river ford — high-clearance recommended for the final stretch.
road.seasonalGate: FR 1570 is unmaintained/rough and subject to washout-driven closures
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, take Cascade River Road east 14.2 miles to FR 1570, stay left at the fork ~0.1 mile in, and park at the next fork 0.4 mile past the river crossing (1.9 miles off Cascade River Road, ~1,250 ft) — the right fork is blocked by boulders.

routes.approach: Hike the Thunder Creek Trail about 2 miles upstream, then turn left onto the Fourth of July Pass trail, climbing roughly 2,000 ft in 2.5 miles to Fourth of July Camp (~3,400 ft) with views of Snowfield Peak and the Neve Glacier. Continue less than a mile to the indistinct, forested pass (~3,600 ft, 5.3 miles from the trailhead), then leave the trail to travel cross-country through Thunder Basin toward the Neve Glacier and Snowfield's summit. This is a glaciated approach requiring crevasse-rescue gear and is typically done as a 2-3 day trip.

road.name: State Route 20 (North Cascades Highway) to Colonial Creek Campground
road.status: Fully paved highway direct to the trailhead
road.seasonalGate: Colonial Creek Campground/Thunder Creek Trailhead sits west of the Ross Dam gate (MP 134); not part of SR-20's standard seasonal high-pass closure, so the trailhead itself is normally reachable by road year-round, snow permitting on the trail
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, follow SR-20 east about 24 miles; just past milepost 130 turn right into Colonial Creek Campground and continue 0.5 mile to the trailhead near the day-use amphitheater.

routes.approach: From the trailhead, hike the Thornton Lakes Trail about 4.5 miles to an overlook (~4,900 ft) above Lower Thornton Lake, then descend to the lake before the trail fades into a boot path continuing to Middle Thornton Lake (~4,700 ft). From the north end of the middle lake, climb a steep, trail-less ravine/gully northwest to a high camp at the 5,840+ ft saddle, the standard base for summit attempts. From camp, climbers typically continue via Triumph's South Ridge or the more difficult Northeast Ridge to the summit.

road.name: Thornton Lakes Road (spur off SR-20)
road.status: Rough, sometimes-potholed gravel road with a steep grade and loose surface; high-clearance and often 4WD strongly recommended, especially after rain
road.seasonalGate: Not gated, but unplowed and typically snow-covered outside roughly June/July through October
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, drive SR-20 east about 11 miles and turn left (north) onto Thornton Lakes Road between mileposts 117 and 118. Follow it roughly 5 miles, climbing over 2,000 ft, to the trailhead (~2,600-3,600 ft).

routes.approach: From the Easy Pass Trailhead, a steep maintained trail climbs about 3.7 miles and 2,800 ft to Easy Pass, then drops into the Fisher Creek basin. From there climbers leave trail, traversing cross-country toward Goode's north side to reach camps used for the Northeast Buttress (the standard route) or the Southwest Couloir (the easiest line). The alternate approach starts from Stehekin via passenger ferry/shuttle to the Park Creek Trailhead, then follows the Park Creek Trail up to Park Creek Pass before contouring to Goode's east side — a shorter hike-in but dependent on the Stehekin shuttle running. Either way this is a multi-day, off-trail alpine objective with real route-finding.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Easy Pass Trailhead (MP 151)
road.status: Open (SR-20 fully paved); the Stehekin Valley Road alternate approach to Park Creek Trailhead has been landslide-affected and its shuttle service is seasonal and conditions-dependent, so treat that route as less reliable
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally for winter (typically late Nov/Dec through April/May) due to snow and avalanche danger over Washington Pass
road.driveNote: Paved highway the entire way; Easy Pass Trailhead is a small pullout (~10 cars) on the south side of SR-20 between mileposts 151-152, about 46 miles east of Marblemount. A Northwest Forest Pass is required.

routes.approach: From the Cascade Pass Trailhead (3,600 ft), a well-maintained trail switchbacks 3.7 miles to Cascade Pass (5,392 ft). From the pass, leave the trail and climb south up Mixup Arm, following benches to roughly 6,000 ft where a small glacier/snowfield requires roping up for glacier travel to reach Gunsight Notch, followed by class 3-4 rock to the summit via the East Face. Mix Up Peak sits at the northern end of the Ptarmigan Traverse, so while a fit party can day-trip it from the pass, most climbers combine it with a longer traverse and camp near Cascade Pass or Pelton Basin.

road.name: Cascade River Road to Cascade Pass Trailhead
road.status: Currently open per NPS; the road is prone to washout damage in heavy rain years, so confirm current status with the park before driving
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated/closed by snow in winter and opens seasonally in late spring/early summer once plowed
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, cross the bridge onto Cascade River Road at SR-20 milepost 106 and drive 23 miles to the road's end. The first ~10 miles are paved; the rest is gravel/washboard requiring high-clearance.

routes.approach: Mount Formidable is a deep objective on the Ptarmigan Traverse, reached from the same Cascade Pass Trailhead as Mix Up Peak but with a much longer approach. From Cascade Pass, descend to Pelton Basin, cross below Cache Col, and traverse over glaciated and talus terrain past Kool-Aid Lake toward Yang Yang Lakes — typically a full day or more of travel with no maintained trail. From a Yang Yang Lakes camp, climbers make a rising traverse to the notch between Formidable and Spider Mountain, cross the Middle Cascade Glacier, and climb the South Route to the summit. This is a full multi-day mountaineering objective requiring glacier travel skills, generally undertaken alongside the Ptarmigan Traverse.

road.name: Cascade River Road to Cascade Pass Trailhead
road.status: Currently open per NPS; the road is prone to washout damage in heavy rain years, so confirm current status with the park before driving
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated/closed by snow in winter and opens seasonally in late spring/early summer once plowed
road.driveNote: From Marblemount, cross the bridge onto Cascade River Road at SR-20 milepost 106 and drive 23 miles to the road's end. The first ~10 miles are paved; the rest is gravel/washboard requiring high-clearance.

routes.approach: No maintained trail reaches the summit. The most-used approach follows the PCT north from Harts Pass over Woody Pass and Lakeview Ridge (~27 trail miles) to Hopkins Lake and Castle Pass, then pushes off-trail west/northwest toward the peak through cross-country talus and brush. Alternate approaches from Freezeout Creek on the Ross Lake NRA side, or from Manning Park in BC, are similarly long and are described by climbers as 'contrived' regardless of direction. Expect 2+ days of hiking before reaching the peak itself; this is a genuinely obscure objective with very few documented trip reports.

road.name: Harts Pass Road (Forest Road 5400) from Mazama
road.status: Steep, narrow, gravel beyond pavement's end, high-clearance recommended
road.seasonalGate: Typically snow-free and open early-mid summer through fall; closed by snow in winter
road.driveNote: From Mazama on SR-20, FR-5400 climbs roughly 20 rugged miles to Harts Pass. This is the standard U.S.-side approach corridor, though Castle Peak itself sits west near the Canadian border; alternate approaches exist via Ross Lake NRA/Hozomeen or from Manning Provincial Park, BC.

routes.approach: From Thirtymile Trailhead, follow the Chewuch River Trail (#510) roughly 17-18 miles north then west, crossing Cathedral Creek before climbing about 2 miles to a high basin near 6,600 ft. A well-built former fire-lookout trail then switchbacks up the talus of Remmel's southeast slope to the summit — a long walk-up with no technical climbing, but a genuine multi-day backpack (~19-20 miles one-way). The Andrews Creek Trailhead offers a somewhat shorter alternate approach into the same Cathedral Lakes/Remmel basin.

road.name: Chewuch River Road (County Rd 1213 to Forest Road 51/5160) from Winthrop
road.status: Generally a maintained paved-then-gravel road to Thirtymile Trailhead; Methow Valley forest roads are subject to seasonal storm and wildfire closures, so current conditions should be checked
road.seasonalGate: Road and trail are typically snow-free July through September; parts of the Chewuch corridor have burned in past wildfires, leaving deadfall on the trail
road.driveNote: From Winthrop, follow West Chewuch Road (Co Rd 1213) about 7 miles, then continue on FR-51/FR-5160 roughly 20+ miles to Thirtymile Trailhead at the road's end, or to Andrews Creek Trailhead a bit earlier off the same road corridor.

routes.approach: Apex Mountain shares its approach with Mount Remmel and Cathedral Peak: from Thirtymile or Andrews Creek Trailhead via the Chewuch River Trail and Tungsten Creek Trail (roughly 10-12 miles) to the Boundary Trail, then about 1.5 miles west/south to Apex Pass. From the pass, leave the trail and climb off-trail through marshy meadows and trees onto the north ridge, following easy class 2 terrain to the 8,297 ft summit. Most parties combine Apex with Remmel, Cathedral, and Amphitheater as part of the roughly 44-mile Cathedral Lakes Loop.

road.name: Chewuch River Road (County Rd 1213 to Forest Road 51/5160) from Winthrop
road.status: Generally a maintained paved-then-gravel road to Thirtymile/Andrews Creek Trailheads; should be checked seasonally for storm/fire closures
road.seasonalGate: Typically snow-free July through September
road.driveNote: Same corridor as Mount Remmel: from Winthrop via West Chewuch Road (Co Rd 1213) onto FR-51/FR-5160 to Thirtymile or Andrews Creek Trailhead.

routes.approach: This name is not a single summit — it likely refers to the glacier/ridge traverse network linking Colonial Peak, Neve Peak, Snowfield Peak, and Isolation Peak (the corridor climbers call the Isolation/Neve Traverse). Standard access is the Thunder Creek Trail from Colonial Creek Campground, climbing past Fourth of July Pass into the Panther Creek basin, then up steep forest and slabs to a high camp around 5,400-5,800 ft. From there parties rope up to cross the Colonial and Neve Glaciers connecting the peaks. These trips typically run 2-4 days and demand full glacier travel gear and crevasse rescue skills; this is a serious, remote alpine mountaineering objective, not a hike.

road.name: North Cascades Highway (SR-20) to Colonial Creek Campground / Thunder Creek Trailhead
road.status: paved, open year-round from the west
road.seasonalGate: SR-20 closes seasonally over Washington Pass (roughly Nov-Apr) well east of here; the Colonial Creek Campground/Thunder Creek trailhead itself is reachable year-round, weather and plowing permitting
road.driveNote: Drive SR-20 east from Sedro-Woolley/Marblemount into Ross Lake NRA to Colonial Creek Campground (MP 130); Thunder Creek Trailhead starts at the south end of the campground.

routes.approach: Southeast ('Hard') Mox Peak is effectively inaccessible from the US in a reasonable trip; almost all climbers approach from British Columbia via Chilliwack Lake Road and the unmaintained Depot Creek climbers' path, which requires a passport and a vehicle border crossing. From the road end, the route follows Depot Creek past Depot Creek Falls, then goes off-trail via scrambling to Ouzel Lake and the Redoubt Glacier saddle before a glacier crossing and Class 5+ rock finish to the summit. It's commonly combined with Redoubt, Spickard, and Northwest (Easy) Mox as the multi-day 'Chilliwack Slam' traverse. This is a remote, technical objective requiring glacier and rock climbing skills.

road.name: Chilliwack Lake Road (BC Forest Service Road) and Depot Creek Road, British Columbia
road.status: unpaved/gravel forest service road, rough and washout-prone; reported washed out past Chilliwack Lake Park after flood damage — verify current status
road.seasonalGate: no fixed seasonal gate, but subject to periodic washout closures
road.driveNote: From the US, cross into Canada, take BC Hwy 1 to Chilliwack, then follow Chilliwack River Road/Chilliwack Lake Road roughly 40 miles toward Chilliwack Lake; turn onto the narrow, unsigned dirt Depot Creek Road near the international border and continue to the road-end trailhead.

routes.approach: Bear Mountain sits deep in the Chilliwack Range and is reached almost entirely on foot from the Hannegan Pass Trailhead: hike over Hannegan Pass, descend the Chilliwack River Trail down-valley, then leave the maintained trail near the Copper Ridge junction and bushwhack/scramble up Bear Mountain's west ridge to a high camp near 4,600 ft. The summit goes via the west ridge and upper north ridge (Class 4-5 scrambling) or the Northwest Ridge, the most commonly cited standard route. Round trips typically run 4-6 days with 30+ miles and well over 10,000 ft of gain.

road.name: Mt Baker Highway (SR-542) to Hannegan Pass Road (FR 32) / Ruth Creek Road
road.status: paved highway to gravel Forest Service road; Hannegan trailhead parking has been reduced by a Ruth Creek washout
road.seasonalGate: gated/snowed-in through winter and early spring; typically opens roughly early summer through fall
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive SR-542 (Mt Baker Highway) east, turn onto FR 32, bear left onto Ruth Creek Road, and continue about 5.4 miles to the road's end at Hannegan Campground/Trailhead.

routes.approach: From the PCT Trailhead at Snoqualmie Pass, follow the Pacific Crest Trail north; the approach to Huckleberry Mountain's summit is roughly 10 miles one-way with substantial elevation gain through Commonwealth Basin toward Kendall Katwalk/Alaska Mountain terrain before leaving the trail for the peak. The final summit block involves stiff class 3-4 scrambling with real exposure, so it's best attempted late July through October when snow has cleared. This is a long day or overnight approach given the mileage.

road.name: I-90 to PCT Trailhead (Commonwealth Basin), Snoqualmie Pass
road.status: open, paved access road, standard trailhead parking lot
road.driveNote: From Seattle, take I-90 east to Exit 52, turn left under the freeway, then take the first right following signs to the PCT Trailhead (Snoqualmie Pass). Northwest Forest Pass required.

routes.approach: The standard approach to Chimney Rock is the Pete Lake Trail, following the Cooper River about 4.5 miles through old-growth forest to Pete Lake, which sits directly below Chimney Rock and Summit Chief Mountain. From there, climbers continue past the lake and pick up climbers' trails toward the Pacific Crest Trail and on to high camps near the Lemah/Chimney glaciers, for a round trip on the order of 16 miles with roughly 4,900 feet of elevation gain. An alternate, much longer approach exists from the north via Necklace Valley/La Bohn Gap off Foss River Road, but that route is a high traverse of ~31 miles round trip and is not the primary access.

road.name: Salmon La Sac Road (WA-903) to Forest Road 46 / FR-4616 (Pete Lake Trailhead)
road.status: open, mix of paved and gravel forest road
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 80 (Roslyn/Salmon La Sac), follow Bullfrog Road through two roundabouts onto WA-903 north through Roslyn and past Cle Elum Lake (~16.8 miles from I-90) to the FR-46 junction. Turn left (west) on FR-46 for 4.7 miles, then right on FR-4616 for about 1 mile to the Pete Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: From the Lake Serene Trailhead, follow the maintained Lake Serene Trail #1068 roughly 4 miles and about 2,000 ft of gain to the lake basin. Traverse around the west side of the lake over talus and boulders to its south end, then climb steep brush and a gully system (snow-covered into early summer) toward the base of the North Peak's North Face. The standard North Face Route is a long, moderate alpine rock climb (Grade III, 5.6-5.7) on featured but sometimes rotten rock with significant brush; parties typically climb it car-to-car in a very long day (12-15+ hours) or with a bivy.

road.name: US-2 to Mount Index Road (Lake Serene / Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead)
road.status: paved to trailhead, generally open
road.driveNote: From US-2 at milepost 35.2 (about 7 miles east of Gold Bar, just west of the Skykomish River bridge), turn south onto Mount Index Road; the signed Lake Serene/Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead is about 0.3 miles up on a paved spur. A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass is required.

routes.approach: Start at the Lake Serene Trailhead and follow Trail #1068 about 4 miles and 2,000 ft of gain to Lake Serene, then traverse talus and boulders around the west side of the lake to its south end. From there head east to a saddle and climb the ridge/gully system southwest of it toward Main Peak, the massif's highest and most technical summit (~5,502 ft). Expect roughly 5,500 ft of total elevation gain, thick brush low on the route, and sustained moderate rock climbing higher up; most parties plan for a 15+ hour car-to-car day or an overnight.

road.name: US-2 to Mount Index Road (Lake Serene / Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead)
road.status: paved to trailhead, generally open
road.driveNote: From US-2 at milepost 35.2 (about 7 miles east of Gold Bar), turn south onto Mount Index Road; the signed Lake Serene/Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead is about 0.3 miles up on a paved spur. A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass is required.

routes.approach: From the Lake Serene Trailhead, hike Trail #1068 about 4 miles and 2,000 ft of gain to Lake Serene, then traverse talus and huge boulders around the lake toward its south/southeast end below the Middle Peak (Jötunheim) massif. This cirque holds the Norwegian Buttresses, home to big-wall routes like Jötnar, one of the tallest walls in the Lower 48; reaching the base involves further talus and brush travel beyond the lake. Given the technical, multi-day nature of routes here, most parties approach with overnight gear.

road.name: US-2 to Mount Index Road (Lake Serene / Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead)
road.status: paved to trailhead, generally open
road.driveNote: From US-2 at milepost 35.2 (about 7 miles east of Gold Bar), turn south onto Mount Index Road; the signed Lake Serene/Bridal Veil Falls Trailhead is about 0.3 miles up on a paved spur. A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass is required.

routes.approach: From the Barclay Lake Trailhead, follow the Barclay Lake Trail roughly one mile to a footbridge, then leave the maintained trail and break into the forest to the right of a large boulder before the creek crossing. An unmarked climbers' path ascends through a boulder field and wash, passing a 'dirty waterfall,' and a steep, root-strewn slope with fixed lines. Above this, the path crosses a forested ridge section and several short rope-assisted pitches through a gully system before reaching the base of the tower — typically 3-4 hours, and easy to lose even with beta.

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (Forest Service Road 6024)
road.status: Gravel forest road, generally passable to standard passenger cars in dry conditions but with recurring potholes and ruts; 4WD/high-clearance recommended during or after wet weather
road.driveNote: From US-2 milepost 42 in Baring, turn north onto FS Road 6024 directly across from the Der Baring Store, cross the railroad tracks, and continue about 4.3 miles to the Barclay Lake Trailhead.

routes.approach: Gunn Peak sits on the north side of the Barclay Creek valley opposite Mount Baring. A common line follows the road back roughly a quarter mile from the trailhead, turns onto an old forest road at an informal campsite, and follows a flagged or boot-beaten path to a log jam crossing, then up overgrown logging-road remnants to a climbers' trail around 3,200 feet where routes to the East Ridge diverge. This is class 3-4 terrain rather than a trail, route-finding is difficult, and brush cover varies significantly year to year.

road.name: Barclay Creek Road (Forest Service Road 6024)
road.status: Same gravel access road as Dolomite Tower/Barclay Lake; potholed and rutted, high-clearance advised in wet conditions
road.driveNote: From US-2 milepost 42 in Baring, turn north onto FS Road 6024 opposite the Der Baring Store, cross the railroad tracks, and drive toward the Barclay Lake Trailhead; some parties park along the road short of the trailhead to pick up the climbers' route on the north side of the valley.

routes.approach: From either trailhead, climbers first gain the Pacific Crest Trail — via the Tunnel Creek Trail climbing about 1,170 ft in 1.45 miles to Hope Lake, or via the Surprise Creek Trail past Surprise and Glacier Lakes — then follow the PCT south several miles to Trap Pass (~5,800 ft). From Trap Pass an unmaintained climbers' trail continues south along the ridge to the base of Slippery Slab Tower, with Thunder Mountain further along the same ridgeline above Glacier Lake. The round trip is long enough (roughly 10+ miles one-way) that many parties camp overnight at Hope Lake or Trap Lake.

road.name: US-2 via Tunnel Creek Trailhead (FS Road 6095) or Surprise Creek Trailhead
road.status: Surprise Creek Trailhead is a paved turnout off US-2 with a short rough dirt spur; Tunnel Creek's access road (FR 6095) is narrow, rutted, and pothole-prone — high-clearance recommended
road.driveNote: Both trailheads are reached directly from US-2 on the west side of Stevens Pass, roughly 13 miles east of the Skykomish turnoff.

routes.approach: From the Hannegan Pass Trailhead, follow Hannegan Pass Trail #674 about 4-4.5 miles and roughly 2,000 ft of gain through forest and up the Ruth Creek valley to Hannegan Pass (5,100 ft). From the pass, leave the maintained trail and climb the ridge toward the base of the Ruth Glacier, then ascend moderate glaciated slopes, weaving between crevasses, to the summit. Round trip is commonly cited at about 12-13.3 miles with roughly 4,000-4,600 ft total elevation gain; a rope, crampons, and glacier gear are standard.

road.name: Hannegan Pass Road (Forest Road 32)
road.status: open
road.seasonalGate: Typically gated/snowed-in in winter and spring; usually clear by early-to-mid summer
road.driveNote: From Glacier, WA, drive SR-542 (Mount Baker Highway) about 13 miles east to Hannegan Pass Road (FR 32), then follow FR 32 roughly 5.3 miles to its end at the Hannegan Pass Trailhead. A Northwest Forest Pass is required, and North Cascades National Park backcountry permits are needed for overnight stays.

routes.approach: Normally, from the Elbow Lake Trailhead the Elbow Lake Trail leads about 1.5 miles past Elbow Lake into the Twin Sisters Range, connecting to climbers' paths up toward the Sisters Divide/Green Glacier area. From there, reaching South Twin Sister typically follows the west ridge or, via a linked traverse from North Twin, crosses the col and ascends the Sisters Glacier to the north ridge and summit. Given the current FR 38 washout, the effective approach is significantly longer than normal, requiring hiking or biking extra distance on damaged road before even reaching the trailhead.

road.name: Middle Fork Nooksack Road (Forest Road 38) to the Elbow Lake Trailhead
road.status: closed/damaged — washed out by Dec 2025 flooding, no repair timeline as of mid-2026
road.seasonalGate: Also seasonally gated to protect elk calving habitat, closed roughly Nov 1-Jul 1, independent of the washout
road.driveNote: From Acme, take Mosquito Lake Road south/east about 9 miles, cross the Middle Fork Nooksack River, then turn onto FR 38 toward the Elbow Lake Trailhead; the road is not considered safely drivable past the washout except on foot, bike, or e-bike.

routes.approach: From the Elbow Lake Trailhead, the trail passes Elbow Lake and continues toward the Twin Sisters/Sisters Divide area, gaining substantial elevation to reach the base of North Twin. The standard route is the West Ridge (moderate alpine rock scrambling on grippy dunite), often linked with South Twin via a traverse across the Sisters Glacier and connecting col. As with South Twin, the current FR 38 washout adds meaningful extra approach distance/time.

road.name: Middle Fork Nooksack Road (Forest Road 38) to the Elbow Lake Trailhead
road.status: closed/damaged — washed out by Dec 2025 flooding, no repair timeline as of mid-2026
road.seasonalGate: Also seasonally gated near Wallace Creek, roughly Nov 1-Jul 1, independent of flood damage
road.driveNote: Same access as South Twin: from Acme, Mosquito Lake Road ~9 miles to the Middle Fork Nooksack crossing, then FR 38 ~11 miles toward the Elbow Lake Trailhead; driving beyond the washout is not advised.

routes.approach: Park at the Reflection Lake pullout on Stevens Canyon Road or at Narada Falls. There is no maintained trail: climbers walk along the road shoulder roughly a half mile to where the road bends sharply east, then leave the pavement and descend cross-country through open forest south toward Tatoosh Creek. From the creek, climbers work up beneath Lane Peak's north face to reach the base of the couloirs (best climbed as snow climbs in spring) or continue around to the Lane-Denman saddle for a scramble route in summer. The off-trail routefinding through the forest and creek drainage is the main challenge, not distance.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road (from Nisqually-Paradise Road/SR 706 via Narada Falls to Reflection Lakes)
road.status: Paved, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens around Memorial Day and closes at the first major snowfall of the season, usually mid-October
road.driveNote: Enter at the Nisqually Entrance and drive SR 706 (Nisqually-Paradise Road) past Longmire to Narada Falls, then continue onto Stevens Canyon Road toward the Reflection Lakes pullouts. Lane Peak's north face is visible directly above the road here.

routes.approach: The Eagle Peak Trailhead is about 300 feet past the suspension bridge on the east side of the Nisqually River. From there, a maintained trail (about 7.2 miles round trip, ~2,955 ft gain) climbs steeply through forest for roughly two miles to a stream crossing, continues about a mile to a meadow, then steepens over rocky terrain for a final half mile to the 5,700-foot Eagle Peak Saddle, where the maintained trail ends. The saddle is exposed with cliff edges that can be corniced with lingering snow into June; routes onto Eagle Peak itself continue past the saddle as a scramble.

road.name: Nisqually-Paradise Road (SR 706) to Longmire
road.status: Paved, open year-round from the Nisqually Entrance to Longmire (weather permitting)
road.driveNote: From the Nisqually Entrance, drive SR 706 to Longmire. In Longmire, pass the National Park Inn, continue through the employee housing area, and cross the historic Nisqually Suspension Bridge (weight-limited to 3 tons).

routes.approach: From the Snow Lake Trailhead, follow the maintained Snow Lake Trail to its end, then leave the trail and climb talus slopes into Unicorn Creek Basin. From the basin, ascend snowfields/couloir to Unicorn Col, cross through the col, and traverse toward Thea Foss Peak before descending on easy grass slopes to Butter Lake, a common camping bench. From Butter Lake, ascend a few hundred feet to the base of The Castle; the standard route climbs an obvious crack-chimney system on the east face. Total trip is roughly 6 miles with about 4,400 feet of elevation gain, typically combined with Unicorn Peak/Pinnacle Peak on the same Tatoosh Range traverse.

road.name: Stevens Canyon Road (from Nisqually-Paradise Road/SR 706 via Narada Falls and Reflection Lakes)
road.status: Paved, open seasonally
road.seasonalGate: Typically opens around Memorial Day and closes at the first major snowfall of the season, usually mid-October
road.driveNote: Enter at the Nisqually Entrance, drive SR 706 through Longmire to Narada Falls, then continue on Stevens Canyon Road past Reflection Lakes (about 1.5 miles) to the Snow Lake Trailhead parking area.

routes.approach: From Randle, drive south on Forest Road 25 to Forest Road 2551, just south of Elk Pass, then east on FR2551 for about 1.6 miles to an unmarked spur on the left; park at or just past the spur. From the parking spur, short flagged connector paths lead to the Boundary Trail. Follow the Boundary Trail 2.25 miles to Badger Lake, then continue 2.75 miles and about 700 feet of elevation gain to the last switchback below the ridge crest; from there an almost-level traverse through open timber breaks out onto the open west face of Shark Rock.

road.name: Forest Road 25 to Forest Road 2551 (Gifford Pinchot National Forest, near Elk Pass)
road.status: open as of late 2025; FR25 has a recent history of landslide-driven closures near Elk Pass/FR2551 junction, so status should be reverified with the Cowlitz Valley Ranger District
road.driveNote: FR2551 is rough but passable washboard/gravel suitable for a standard high-clearance car; no formal winter gate confirmed, but as a high-elevation Cascades gravel road it is snow-blocked outside roughly summer/early fall.

routes.approach: From the Fryingpan Creek Trailhead (~3,800 ft), hike the Fryingpan Creek Trail about 4.1 miles and 2,100 ft of gain to Summerland. From Summerland, leave the maintained trail and climb cross-country west to Meany Crest (~7,573 ft), cross the Fryingpan Glacier southwest to Whitman Crest (~9,323 ft), descend onto the Whitman Glacier, and finish with 400-800 ft of class 2-3 rock scrambling to Little Tahoma's summit (11,138 ft) — roughly 7 miles and 7,300+ ft one-way, with real rockfall hazard on the upper Fryingpan Glacier. The same NPS Climbing Cost Recovery fee and permit apply for glacier travel and any route above 10,000 ft.

road.name: White River Road (via SR 410 and the White River/Sunrise Entrance)
road.status: Paved but seasonal — only open roughly late May/June through mid-October
road.seasonalGate: Entire road to Sunrise/White River closes for winter by mid-October or with the first heavy snowfall and does not reopen until early summer
road.driveNote: From Seattle: take WA-410 E through Enumclaw, then continue on SR 410 roughly 43 miles to the White River Entrance — about 90 miles, ~2 hours. Inside the park, follow White River Road about 3 miles to the Fryingpan Creek parking area.

routes.approach: From the Cold Springs Trailhead (5,600 ft), the South Climb Trail #183 leads up through forest and open slopes to Lunch Counter on Suksdorf Ridge, then continues up steep snow/scree past the false summit (Pikers Peak) to the true 12,281 ft summit, roughly 6.7 miles one-way with about 6,700 ft of gain. Climbers 16+ need a Mt. Adams Climbing Pass (booked via Recreation.gov) any time they travel above 7,000 ft between May 1 and September 30. The climb typically takes 6-8 hours up and 4-6 hours down; parking at the trailhead is limited.

road.name: Forest Road 8040 (via FR-23 and FR-80) from Trout Lake
road.status: Bumpy, rutted gravel with washboard sections; good clearance and AWD required, 4WD recommended
road.seasonalGate: No formal gate, but typically snow-blocked until roughly late May-June depending on snowpack
road.driveNote: From Trout Lake, WA take Mt. Adams Rd/FR-23 north about 1 mile to the junction with FR-80 (signed for South Climb), turn left and follow FR-80 for 3.7 miles to FR-8040, turn right and follow FR-8040 for 8.1 miles to its end at the Cold Springs/South Climb Trailhead (5,600 ft).

routes.approach: From Climbers Bivouac, the Ptarmigan Trail #216A leads into the Monitor Ridge climbing route, gaining roughly 4,500 ft over about 5 miles one-way to the crater rim at 8,365 ft (the summit is not entered due to volcanic hazard). A climbing permit is required year-round above 4,800 ft; during the quota season (April 1-October 31) permits are sold in advance on Recreation.gov, released one month ahead, with daily caps of 350 (April 1-May 14) and 110 (May 15-October 31).

road.name: Forest Road 83 to Forest Spur Road 830, via Cougar, WA
road.status: FR-83 is paved but rough (use caution); FR-830 is a winding 2.7-mile gravel spur; road and lot unsuitable for RVs or trailers
road.seasonalGate: No formal gate, but the route to Climbers Bivouac is generally closed by snow in winter and typically opens by late spring, around when the quota season begins April 1
road.driveNote: From Cougar, WA take Lewis River Rd/FR-90 east 6.4 miles, turn left onto FR-83 and follow it about 3 miles, stay left onto FR-81 for about 1 mile to FR-830, then turn right and drive 2.7 miles to the Climbers Bivouac trailhead.
